title: #eng-sre: 1768680002
preview:
Rafael Mendes: hey folks — need quick eyes on the customer comms draft for the planned regional failover test next week. want to lock wording + timing + escalation contacts today so Support isn’t improvising.

Rafael Mendes: draft is here (Google doc): https://drive.google.com/redwood/failover/customer-comms-draft-planned-failover

Kira Thompson: skimming now. first reaction: we say “no customer impact expected” in the first paragraph. i’d soften that.

Kira Thompson: maybe “we do not expect significant impact, but you may see brief increases in latency or transient errors during the window.”

Dev Patel: +1. Support gets burned when we use absolutes.

Owen Phillips: agree. can we also define what “brief” means? like “<2 minutes” if that’s true, or just “short-lived”.

Rafael Mendes: good call. we *hope* <60s but don’t want to promise. i can say “short-lived” and keep the quantitative bits internal.

Tomáš Novák: also the draft says “test is fully automated.” is that accurate? we still have approval hooks in prod, right?

Rafael Mendes: yep, approvals required for execution. automation does detection + recommendation; on-call acknowledges. will tweak.

Sean Gallagher: +1. we should frame it as “controlled” and “with active monitoring + rollback.”

Lauren Bishop: dedicated customers will ask “are you draining my pool?” draft implies routing-only.

Lauren Bishop: can we add a Dedicated-specific line: “Dedicated capacity will remain reserved; any drain/evacuation would be performed only if required for safety and with our on-call team engaged.”

Rafael Mendes: yep. we’re not planning a drain step for Dedicated tenants in this test, but we need to preempt the question.

Nikhil Sharma: hosted API section: we mention “automatic retries will mask impact.” careful — some clients don’t retry and we shouldn’t tell them to rely on it.

Nikhil Sharma: maybe “our platform includes safeguards (routing, circuit breakers) to reduce impact” without mentioning client behavior.

Allison Grant: +1. also if we mention retries we should link to docs. otherwise it’s vague + potentially wrong.

Kira Thompson: i’ll propose edit:

Kira Thompson: 
“During this maintenance window, we will run a controlled regional failover exercise to validate our reliability systems. We do not expect significant customer impact, but you may observe short-lived increases in latency or transient HTTP 5xx/timeout errors.”

Kira Thompson: then next sentence: “If you experience sustained impact outside the window, contact Support and reference ‘Planned Failover Test’.”

Dev Patel: for escalation contacts, can we include both the support portal and the enterprise escalation alias?

Dev Patel: Support asks for:
- support@redwoodinference.com (or portal)
- for enterprise: their CSM channel + “SEV escalation” process

Owen Phillips: and internal: we should tell customers we’ll update status page if we see meaningful impact. but don’t overcommit to “every 15 minutes” unless that’s policy.

Tomáš Novák: status page cadence standard is 30m for ongoing events, right? for planned test we can do one “start” + “complete” unless issues.

Rafael Mendes: yeah. we have the template. will align with status-page copy.

Talia Benson: timing: draft says “Jan 15 10:00–10:30 PT”. is that final? i thought we were still picking between Tue/Wed based on infra signoff.

Rafael Mendes: you’re right. i’ll change to “<DATE> <TIME>” placeholders until go/no-go. but we need something to send to the enterprise list once approved.

Sean Gallagher: for enterprise proactive notify: which accounts? i’d keep to customers with multi-region routing enabled + big traffic, and any who have asked about HA.

Dev Patel: Support list suggestion: Atlas Payments (prospect but in pilot), a couple of top 10 usage hosted accounts, and any dedicated customers in us-east/eu-west.

Lauren Bishop: +1 on dedicated accounts. some will need a heads up even if no impact expected.

Marissa Cole: wording: “This test will improve regional resiliency” is good, but also say it’s part of reliability program + we run these periodically.

Marissa Cole: sets expectation that planned drills are normal.

Allison Grant: can we add a line “we will monitor success criteria and immediately rollback if thresholds exceeded” (without giving the thresholds)?

Rafael Mendes: yep.

Kira Thompson: also there’s a sentence “failover between us-east → eu-west” — might cause data residency questions.

Kira Thompson: should we add “routing respects customer data residency and regional constraints” (esp for regulated tiers).

Rafael Mendes: good catch. we can say: “Failover behavior respects configured regional boundaries; customers with regional constraints will not be routed outside allowed regions.”

Tomáš Novák: +1. and avoid naming specific regions in generic customer-facing note unless it’s targeted comms.

Nikhil Sharma: agree. for general banner/status-page, keep it generic: “one of our regions.” for targeted emails to impacted cohort, we can include details.

Owen Phillips: Support playbook ask: include “what to tell customer if they report errors during window” (ack + ask for request ids + note planned test). can we add a short FAQ?

Dev Patel: yes please. FAQ items:
- what errors might i see?
- do i need to do anything?
- how to reach support?
- will you provide report after?

Rafael Mendes: ok. Kira/Elliot can you own FAQ + copy changes?

Elliot Price: yep. i’ll update the doc and also mirror to Confluence once final so it’s easy to find.

Elliot Price: do we want to reference the public status page URL or just “our status page”? some customers paste links in tickets.

Dev Patel: include the URL. reduces back-and-forth.

Sean Gallagher: approval chain: who signs off on outbound? i assume Neha/Sean + Support lead.

Rafael Mendes: yes. we’ll do: SRE (me) + Sean + Support escalation owner (Dev) before sending. for enterprise-targeted emails, loop in CSM.

Tomáš Novák: add internal instruction: “If customer escalates as SEV1 during the window, route to #incidents + page SRE on-call; reference change window ID.”

Rafael Mendes: good. we’ll add internal-only section (not in customer email) + link it from runbook.

Allison Grant: also: mention where customers can check current region routing? Console banner mock might cover it but not sure it ships before test.

Nikhil Sharma: console banner isn’t guaranteed. i’d avoid promising console visibility. maybe “Support can confirm current mitigation status” instead.

Rafael Mendes: agreed.

Kira Thompson: i’ll make edits now. will ping here for final review.

Elliot Price: updating doc with the changes discussed + adding FAQ. ETA 30 mins.

Dev Patel: once updated, i’ll sanity-check for Support tone and add the exact escalation wording we use in tickets.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: goal: final by EOD so Talia can slot it into the go/no-go packet.
text:
Rafael Mendes: hey folks — need quick eyes on the customer comms draft for the planned regional failover test next week. want to lock wording + timing + escalation contacts today so Support isn’t improvising.

Rafael Mendes: draft is here (Google doc): https://drive.google.com/redwood/failover/customer-comms-draft-planned-failover

Kira Thompson: skimming now. first reaction: we say “no customer impact expected” in the first paragraph. i’d soften that.

Kira Thompson: maybe “we do not expect significant impact, but you may see brief increases in latency or transient errors during the window.”

Dev Patel: +1. Support gets burned when we use absolutes.

Owen Phillips: agree. can we also define what “brief” means? like “<2 minutes” if that’s true, or just “short-lived”.

Rafael Mendes: good call. we *hope* <60s but don’t want to promise. i can say “short-lived” and keep the quantitative bits internal.

Tomáš Novák: also the draft says “test is fully automated.” is that accurate? we still have approval hooks in prod, right?

Rafael Mendes: yep, approvals required for execution. automation does detection + recommendation; on-call acknowledges. will tweak.

Sean Gallagher: +1. we should frame it as “controlled” and “with active monitoring + rollback.”

Lauren Bishop: dedicated customers will ask “are you draining my pool?” draft implies routing-only.

Lauren Bishop: can we add a Dedicated-specific line: “Dedicated capacity will remain reserved; any drain/evacuation would be performed only if required for safety and with our on-call team engaged.”

Rafael Mendes: yep. we’re not planning a drain step for Dedicated tenants in this test, but we need to preempt the question.

Nikhil Sharma: hosted API section: we mention “automatic retries will mask impact.” careful — some clients don’t retry and we shouldn’t tell them to rely on it.

Nikhil Sharma: maybe “our platform includes safeguards (routing, circuit breakers) to reduce impact” without mentioning client behavior.

Allison Grant: +1. also if we mention retries we should link to docs. otherwise it’s vague + potentially wrong.

Kira Thompson: i’ll propose edit:

Kira Thompson: 
“During this maintenance window, we will run a controlled regional failover exercise to validate our reliability systems. We do not expect significant customer impact, but you may observe short-lived increases in latency or transient HTTP 5xx/timeout errors.”

Kira Thompson: then next sentence: “If you experience sustained impact outside the window, contact Support and reference ‘Planned Failover Test’.”

Dev Patel: for escalation contacts, can we include both the support portal and the enterprise escalation alias?

Dev Patel: Support asks for:
- support@redwoodinference.com (or portal)
- for enterprise: their CSM channel + “SEV escalation” process

Owen Phillips: and internal: we should tell customers we’ll update status page if we see meaningful impact. but don’t overcommit to “every 15 minutes” unless that’s policy.

Tomáš Novák: status page cadence standard is 30m for ongoing events, right? for planned test we can do one “start” + “complete” unless issues.

Rafael Mendes: yeah. we have the template. will align with status-page copy.

Talia Benson: timing: draft says “Jan 15 10:00–10:30 PT”. is that final? i thought we were still picking between Tue/Wed based on infra signoff.

Rafael Mendes: you’re right. i’ll change to “<DATE> <TIME>” placeholders until go/no-go. but we need something to send to the enterprise list once approved.

Sean Gallagher: for enterprise proactive notify: which accounts? i’d keep to customers with multi-region routing enabled + big traffic, and any who have asked about HA.

Dev Patel: Support list suggestion: Atlas Payments (prospect but in pilot), a couple of top 10 usage hosted accounts, and any dedicated customers in us-east/eu-west.

Lauren Bishop: +1 on dedicated accounts. some will need a heads up even if no impact expected.

Marissa Cole: wording: “This test will improve regional resiliency” is good, but also say it’s part of reliability program + we run these periodically.

Marissa Cole: sets expectation that planned drills are normal.

Allison Grant: can we add a line “we will monitor success criteria and immediately rollback if thresholds exceeded” (without giving the thresholds)?

Rafael Mendes: yep.

Kira Thompson: also there’s a sentence “failover between us-east → eu-west” — might cause data residency questions.

Kira Thompson: should we add “routing respects customer data residency and regional constraints” (esp for regulated tiers).

Rafael Mendes: good catch. we can say: “Failover behavior respects configured regional boundaries; customers with regional constraints will not be routed outside allowed regions.”

Tomáš Novák: +1. and avoid naming specific regions in generic customer-facing note unless it’s targeted comms.

Nikhil Sharma: agree. for general banner/status-page, keep it generic: “one of our regions.” for targeted emails to impacted cohort, we can include details.

Owen Phillips: Support playbook ask: include “what to tell customer if they report errors during window” (ack + ask for request ids + note planned test). can we add a short FAQ?

Dev Patel: yes please. FAQ items:
- what errors might i see?
- do i need to do anything?
- how to reach support?
- will you provide report after?

Rafael Mendes: ok. Kira/Elliot can you own FAQ + copy changes?

Elliot Price: yep. i’ll update the doc and also mirror to Confluence once final so it’s easy to find.

Elliot Price: do we want to reference the public status page URL or just “our status page”? some customers paste links in tickets.

Dev Patel: include the URL. reduces back-and-forth.

Sean Gallagher: approval chain: who signs off on outbound? i assume Neha/Sean + Support lead.

Rafael Mendes: yes. we’ll do: SRE (me) + Sean + Support escalation owner (Dev) before sending. for enterprise-targeted emails, loop in CSM.

Tomáš Novák: add internal instruction: “If customer escalates as SEV1 during the window, route to #incidents + page SRE on-call; reference change window ID.”

Rafael Mendes: good. we’ll add internal-only section (not in customer email) + link it from runbook.

Allison Grant: also: mention where customers can check current region routing? Console banner mock might cover it but not sure it ships before test.

Nikhil Sharma: console banner isn’t guaranteed. i’d avoid promising console visibility. maybe “Support can confirm current mitigation status” instead.

Rafael Mendes: agreed.

Kira Thompson: i’ll make edits now. will ping here for final review.

Elliot Price: updating doc with the changes discussed + adding FAQ. ETA 30 mins.

Dev Patel: once updated, i’ll sanity-check for Support tone and add the exact escalation wording we use in tickets.

title: #eng-sre: 1768671100
preview:
Miles Thornton: need to settle this: do we require manual approval hooks for prod failover controller, or go fully auto once signals trip?

Rafael Mendes: for initial rollout i strongly prefer approval in prod. we *just* got past the noisy signal issue in staging. last thing we need is a thundering herd because controller flipped twice.

Benji Okafor: +1 to approval as default, but i’d like us to be specific about what “approval” means. on-call click button? slack /ack? timeboxed hold? otherwise it becomes theater.

Mina Haddad: from routing side: full automation is the end state. manual approval doesn’t scale and we’ll miss SLOs if we wait for a human at 3am. but i’m ok with phased.

Allison Grant: FYI dashboards/alerts can support either. bigger issue is: can on-call reliably tell it’s a real region impairment vs a localized 5xx spike? if not, approval just delays and still fails.

Ravi Menon: policy engine semantics matter here. if controller is deterministic + has corroboration (reachability + burn) + hold-down timers, the risk is much lower. the most dangerous mode is partial data → it “thinks” region dead and flips.

Tomáš Novák: also paging hygiene angle: if we add an approval step, we need a clear runbook + a single pager path. otherwise we page SRE + routing + infra every time and everyone waits for everyone.

Peter Holtz: gateway behavior matters too. if we’re still retrying too aggressively, an auto flip can amplify load on the target region before autoscaling catches up. approval hook gives us a chance to pre-warm / shed.

Miles Thornton: ok, but approval adds detection→failover time. our goal was to shrink it materially. how do we reconcile?

Rafael Mendes: proposal: approval required only when blast radius > X or confidence < Y. if it’s a “hard” signal (cross-region reachability failing from multiple probes) AND burn is high, controller can auto.

Benji Okafor: that’s basically “progressive automation.” define tiers:
- Tier 0: dry-run only (emit recommendation + evidence)
- Tier 1: auto-exec for low-risk routes/tenants, approval for the rest
- Tier 2: auto-exec broadly, approval only for enterprise / residency constrained

Allison Grant: we need to define what confidence means. i can compute a “corroboration count” (e.g., 2/3 signals) and surface it. but it needs to map to actual rules.

Ravi Menon: ADR-042 already has the hierarchy: reachability > 5xx burn > tail latency burn > capacity. the question is: do we require *reachability* for auto exec? i vote yes.

Mina Haddad: careful: reachability probes can be flaky too (esp. if provider is doing weird DNS). but i agree it should be required for fully automatic regional flip. latency burn alone shouldn’t flip prod.

Peter Holtz: +1. latency is noisy and can be workload-dependent. 5xx burn is stronger but can still be caused by a single service. reachability from canaries + real traffic is best.

Devon Price: from incident-bot side, approval hook is extra moving parts. but we can make it sane: controller posts a summary to #incidents + #eng-sre with buttons/commands:
`/failover approve <event_id>`
`/failover deny <event_id> reason=<...>`
then it writes audit log.

Tomáš Novák: pls no “button in slack but also in console but also in cli”. choose one primary for on-call.

Benji Okafor: agree. CLI is fine for rehearsals, slack for prod ack is probably fastest.

Miles Thornton: what are the conditions to REMOVE approvals entirely? we need criteria we can tell leadership.

Rafael Mendes: measurable criteria IMO:
1) false positive rate below threshold (e.g., <1 per quarter) in prod for routes in scope
2) successful game day(s) with auto-exec enabled on limited blast radius and no manual intervention
3) rollback proven: auto rollback triggers only when impairment cleared and does not oscillate
4) capacity gates working: target region headroom >= N% and no overload cascade
5) audit + observability complete (timeline annotations, event logs)

Allison Grant: add: alerting noise tuned. if auto-exec fires but we still page 5 teams, it’s not a win.

Mina Haddad: also: deterministic policy evaluation + “missing signal behavior” fixed. we had that NPE when a signal stream dropped. approvals won’t save us if controller crashes mid-event.

Benji Okafor: per-tenant safety limits must be on before we remove approvals:
- max flips per tenant per hour
- global max flips per region per hour
- explicit denylist (residency / regulated)

Ravi Menon: and config freeze / change control. if someone edits thresholds at noon and it auto flips at 12:05, that’s on us. we should gate config changes with review + maybe a cool-down.

Peter Holtz: plus the client behavior: streaming reconnect fix is landing, but until then, auto flips can look worse than they are because clients don’t retry correctly. approval doesn’t fix but might reduce frequency.

Devon Price: re: auditability, we can log:
- trigger signals (top 3)
- decision path (which rule fired)
- action (flip/rollback/noop)
- actor (auto vs @oncall)
- blast radius estimate
and link to dashboards.

Sean Gallagher: jumping in. we’re not deciding “forever.” the reliability program expectation is phased. leadership will accept approvals initially if we can show a clear path to removing them.

Neha Kapoor: +1. also want a crisp statement: “automation should be safe-by-default.” if approvals remain indefinitely, that’s a failure. but we won’t trade safety for speed.

Miles Thornton: ok, what’s the compromise policy for Feb rollout?

Rafael Mendes: my vote:
- default approval required in prod
- auto-exec allowed only for: Hosted API, non-enterprise tiers, limited set of routes/models, AND only when reachability corroborated by >=2 probe sources + 5xx burn > fast threshold
- for Dedicated: approval always required until drain automation + capacity validation are solid

Mina Haddad: can live with that if we set a date/exit criteria. otherwise it’ll linger.

Benji Okafor: suggest we encode it as feature flags:
`failover.auto_exec.enabled=true/false`
`failover.auto_exec.allowed_tiers=starter,pro`
`failover.auto_exec.required_signals=reachability_multi,5xx_burn`
`failover.auto_exec.max_blast_radius_pct=10`

Allison Grant: if we’re doing blast radius caps, we need the metric for “traffic shifted” in near real time (per region per route). i can add a panel + alert when it exceeds cap.

Tomáš Novák: also runbook: who has authority to approve/deny? primary on-call only? do we require IC during an incident? and what about planned game days?

Devon Price: for game days, we can pre-authorize via a change ticket and allow auto-exec within a maintenance window. bot can post “pre-approved window active” message.

Peter Holtz: one more guardrail: if target region is already in overload (queue depth high / shed active), controller should refuse to flip and require manual.

Ravi Menon: yep, capacity signal should be a gate (not a trigger). “don’t fail over into fire.”

Miles Thornton: so conditions to remove approval:
- 2 successful production drills with auto-exec on limited routes
- <1 false positive over 30 days
- capacity gate + per-tenant safety limits + anti-flap timers enabled
- audit logs + dashboard annotations confirmed
- streaming reconnect fix deployed
sound right?

Rafael Mendes: add: we need an explicit rollback play: if automation flips and things worsen, on-call should be able to “pin back” and disable automation quickly.

Benji Okafor: kill switch has to be single command. like `/failover disable --scope prod`.

Devon Price: can do. bot can expose `/failover disable` and it flips the feature flag + posts confirmation.

Allison Grant: pls make sure disable action also annotates dashboards. we missed annotations last rehearsal and it was impossible to line up timelines.

Sean Gallagher: action item: Miles + Benji draft the approval policy text for ADR-043 addendum (or a short RFC) + Rafael updates runbook to reflect slack-ack flow. we’ll review in next reliability sync.

Neha Kapoor: and include the exit criteria in that doc. i want it explicit: “approvals removed when X/Y/Z.”

Miles Thornton: ack. i’ll draft today.

Tomáš Novák: once drafted, tag me. i’ll sanity-check the on-call ergonomics + paging.

Mina Haddad: same, i’ll confirm it doesn’t conflict with routing rollout flags.

Peter Holtz: and i’ll verify it aligns with gateway circuit breaker tuning. :thumbsup:

Devon Price: i’ll prototype the `/failover approve` + `/failover disable` commands in incident-bot this week and share screenshots.

Rafael Mendes: cool. also: please make “deny” require a reason string. future us will thank you.

Devon Price: done.

Miles Thornton: closing loop: we’re not choosing manual forever; we’re choosing a ramp with explicit criteria. thanks all.

title: #eng-sre: 1768634300
preview:
Rafael Mendes: heads up — we got paged 6 times overnight on the new multi-region burn-rate rules. mostly recovered by the time someone opened the dash. feels noisy.

PagerDuty: Triggered: [P1] multi_region_availability_burn_rate_fast (service=hosted-api, region=eu-west) — https://pagerduty.redwood.ai/incidents/PQ7K2

Tomáš Novák: yeah, that was me at 02:14. opened the failover dash and it looked green-ish except a small blip on `/v1/chat.completions`. still paged.

Allison Grant: can someone paste the alert annotation from the firing? want to see which clause tripped (fast vs slow window).

Sergio Costa: from Alertmanager:
```
alertname=multi_region_availability_burn_rate_fast
scope=hosted
route_group=default
region=eu-west
window=5m
burnrate=17.8
threshold=14
```
Also slow window was *not* firing.

Miles Thornton: 17.8x over 5m with slow not corroborating sounds like either a brief 5xx spike or a partial gateway flap. We said in ADR-042 we shouldn’t page on single-window spikes w/o corroboration.

Aisha Bello: +1. for multiwindow we should require 5m+30m (or 5m+1h) both burning before paging. right now the fast one pages on its own.

Rafael Mendes: yep. current rule is basically:
- fast: 14x over 5m => page
- slow: 6x over 30m => page
(no AND)

Allison Grant: that’s the old template behavior. I think when we rushed the rule bundle we lost the multiwindow gating. We also added region label + route_group label so it’s a lot of potential alert instances.

Sergio Costa: fwiw last night was also a deploy to `api-gateway` in eu-west + perf-canary was flapping. possible causal.

Observability Bot: Grafana snapshot (auto): Failover Signals / eu-west / Hosted
- 5xx rate peaked at 1.9% for ~3 min
- p99 latency spiked to 4.2s for ~4 min
- reachability probes: OK
https://grafana.redwood.ai/s/failover-eu-west-1768634

Tomáš Novák: yep that matches what I saw. by the time I joined, error rate was back <0.2%. paging on a 3 min blip is rough.

Miles Thornton: agreed. we want fast detection for real regional impairment, but a 3-min spike shouldn’t wake someone unless it’s sustained / corroborated.

Allison Grant: proposal:
1) switch paging to multiwindow AND for “fast”: (14x over 5m) AND (6x over 30m)
2) keep (6x over 30m) as ticket-only if it fires alone
3) add 10m hold-down before auto-resolve so we don’t get fire/resolve spam

Sergio Costa: +1, and also consider grouping — page at region level, not per `route_group`. route_group should be in annotations/dashboard link only.

Rafael Mendes: question: do we still need per-model/route granularity anywhere? feels like cardinality trap.

Aisha Bello: we should keep per-route in *metrics* (for debugging) but alerts should aggregate to “region impairment candidate”. In PromQL that means something like `sum without(route_group, model)`.

Allison Grant: yep. current expression is `sum by (region, route_group) (...)`.

Tomáš Novák: can we also add a “don’t page if under N rps” guard? some of the staging-ish tenants cause high burn on tiny denominators.

Sergio Costa: careful — for SLO burn, denominator is requests, so low volume can be weird. We can clamp by requiring `requests_total > X` in the window. Alternatively, page only on `scope=prod` + `tier in (hosted, dedicated)` and exclude `sandbox`.

Rafael Mendes: prod only is fine. This was prod though. But I like the idea of a min traffic gate so a single tenant doesn’t dominate.

Miles Thornton: for regional impairment detection, a min-traffic gate makes sense, but we also need to catch “everything dead” scenarios where traffic drops to ~0 because clients can’t connect.

Allison Grant: right — if traffic goes to zero due to reachability, the burn metric won’t even move. That’s why reachability is higher priority anyway. So min-traffic gate on burn alerts is ok as long as reachability is paging separately.

Sergio Costa: reachability alerts are separate (perf-canary + external probe). Those did not fire.

Devon Price: fyi incident-bot summary posts when failover controller flips routes. last night there were no flips. so the page was purely signal noise.

Rafael Mendes: ok so concrete changes:
- multiwindow AND for paging
- aggregate out route_group
- add hold-down + maybe min traffic
Can someone own a PR to the alert bundle? (observability-pack)

Sergio Costa: I can take it. will update rules in `observability-pack/pr-907` follow-up. target today.

Allison Grant: please include before/after replay using yesterday’s data if you can (use the simulate endpoint recordings?). we should verify we still would have paged on the jan 15 real impairment.

Sergio Costa: yep, I’ll run it against the recorded timeseries from 2026-01-15 + last night’s blip.

Tomáš Novák: also paging routing: can we downgrade slow window to non-page (Slack + ticket) by default? on-call can escalate if it’s trending.

Miles Thornton: +1. for failover automation, we care about fast confirmable signals. slow burn is for “things are bad, look soon” not wakeups.

Aisha Bello: suggested mapping:
- fast AND (multiwindow) => PagerDuty P1
- slow only => Slack #oncall-sre + Jira ticket
- annotations include direct link to “region health evaluation logs” (PR-19835?)

Allison Grant: love that. also add runbook link in annotations: `hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook`.

Sergio Costa: ack. Draft rule shape:
```
# page only if fast + slow
(BR_5m > 14) and (BR_30m > 6)
# aggregate by region, scope
sum without(route_group, model, tenant) (...)
# min traffic
and (requests_30m > 5000)
```
Plus keep reachability separate.

Rafael Mendes: `requests_30m > 5000` seems high for some smaller regions at night. can we do per-region baseline? or just lower?

Aisha Bello: maybe `> 1000` and we can tune. also we can make it “or dedicated_pool_traffic > 200” since dedicated has lower rps but high impact.

Miles Thornton: keep it simple: start with 1000 and watch.

Allison Grant: +1. and add a note that if traffic is low, we rely more on reachability + latency + control plane health.

PagerDuty: Resolved: [P1] multi_region_availability_burn_rate_fast (service=hosted-api, region=eu-west)

Tomáš Novák: lol resolved while we’re here.

Sergio Costa: ok I’ll ship changes w/ feature flag in alert bundle so we can roll out without surprises.

Rafael Mendes: cool. pls post diff + new thresholds here before merge.

---

Sergio Costa: update: pushed branch `failover-burn-multiwindow-gated`.
Changes:
- paging condition now requires BOTH 5m and 30m burn
- removed `route_group` from grouping
- slow-only now routes to Slack + ticket
- annotations include runbook + dashboard link
PR incoming in ~30.

Allison Grant: can you paste the new alert annotation text? want to ensure it explains the evidence.

Sergio Costa: annotation draft:
"Multi-region availability burn indicates possible regional impairment. Evidence: 5m burn={{$value_fast}}, 30m burn={{$value_slow}}, req_30m={{$req}}. Check Failover Signals dashboard + Region Health logs. Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/.../hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook"

Devon Price: can we also add link to incident-bot thread search? like `slack://channel?team=...&id=incidents` isn’t great. maybe just link to incident timeline tool.

Sergio Costa: I’ll link to incident timeline in console: https://ops.redwood.ai/timeline?region={{$labels.region}}

Aisha Bello: did the replay test show it would have paged on jan 15?

Sergio Costa: yes. jan 15 had sustained 5xx for ~18m, both windows burned, would page. last night would NOT page (only 5m spiked).

Miles Thornton: perfect.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: merge once approved. and let’s add a follow-up task to revisit thresholds after next gameday.

Tomáš Novák: +1 and please add to paging hygiene notes: “single-window spikes should not page” so we don’t regress later.

Allison Grant: I’ll add a short section to the dashboard spec + runbook.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. closing loop: goal is fewer wakeups but still detect real regional events fast. this seems aligned.
text:
Rafael Mendes: heads up — we got paged 6 times overnight on the new multi-region burn-rate rules. mostly recovered by the time someone opened the dash. feels noisy.

PagerDuty: Triggered: [P1] multi_region_availability_burn_rate_fast (service=hosted-api, region=eu-west) — https://pagerduty.redwood.ai/incidents/PQ7K2

Tomáš Novák: yeah, that was me at 02:14. opened the failover dash and it looked green-ish except a small blip on `/v1/chat.completions`. still paged.

Allison Grant: can someone paste the alert annotation from the firing? want to see which clause tripped (fast vs slow window).

Sergio Costa: from Alertmanager:
```
alertname=multi_region_availability_burn_rate_fast
scope=hosted
route_group=default
region=eu-west
window=5m
burnrate=17.8
threshold=14
```
Also slow window was *not* firing.

Miles Thornton: 17.8x over 5m with slow not corroborating sounds like either a brief 5xx spike or a partial gateway flap. We said in ADR-042 we shouldn’t page on single-window spikes w/o corroboration.

Aisha Bello: +1. for multiwindow we should require 5m+30m (or 5m+1h) both burning before paging. right now the fast one pages on its own.

Rafael Mendes: yep. current rule is basically:
- fast: 14x over 5m => page
- slow: 6x over 30m => page
(no AND)

Allison Grant: that’s the old template behavior. I think when we rushed the rule bundle we lost the multiwindow gating. We also added region label + route_group label so it’s a lot of potential alert instances.

Sergio Costa: fwiw last night was also a deploy to `api-gateway` in eu-west + perf-canary was flapping. possible causal.

Observability Bot: Grafana snapshot (auto): Failover Signals / eu-west / Hosted
- 5xx rate peaked at 1.9% for ~3 min
- p99 latency spiked to 4.2s for ~4 min
- reachability probes: OK
https://grafana.redwood.ai/s/failover-eu-west-1768634

Tomáš Novák: yep that matches what I saw. by the time I joined, error rate was back <0.2%. paging on a 3 min blip is rough.

Miles Thornton: agreed. we want fast detection for real regional impairment, but a 3-min spike shouldn’t wake someone unless it’s sustained / corroborated.

Allison Grant: proposal:
1) switch paging to multiwindow AND for “fast”: (14x over 5m) AND (6x over 30m)
2) keep (6x over 30m) as ticket-only if it fires alone
3) add 10m hold-down before auto-resolve so we don’t get fire/resolve spam

Sergio Costa: +1, and also consider grouping — page at region level, not per `route_group`. route_group should be in annotations/dashboard link only.

Rafael Mendes: question: do we still need per-model/route granularity anywhere? feels like cardinality trap.

Aisha Bello: we should keep per-route in *metrics* (for debugging) but alerts should aggregate to “region impairment candidate”. In PromQL that means something like `sum without(route_group, model)`.

Allison Grant: yep. current expression is `sum by (region, route_group) (...)`.

Tomáš Novák: can we also add a “don’t page if under N rps” guard? some of the staging-ish tenants cause high burn on tiny denominators.

Sergio Costa: careful — for SLO burn, denominator is requests, so low volume can be weird. We can clamp by requiring `requests_total > X` in the window. Alternatively, page only on `scope=prod` + `tier in (hosted, dedicated)` and exclude `sandbox`.

Rafael Mendes: prod only is fine. This was prod though. But I like the idea of a min traffic gate so a single tenant doesn’t dominate.

Miles Thornton: for regional impairment detection, a min-traffic gate makes sense, but we also need to catch “everything dead” scenarios where traffic drops to ~0 because clients can’t connect.

Allison Grant: right — if traffic goes to zero due to reachability, the burn metric won’t even move. That’s why reachability is higher priority anyway. So min-traffic gate on burn alerts is ok as long as reachability is paging separately.

Sergio Costa: reachability alerts are separate (perf-canary + external probe). Those did not fire.

Devon Price: fyi incident-bot summary posts when failover controller flips routes. last night there were no flips. so the page was purely signal noise.

Rafael Mendes: ok so concrete changes:
- multiwindow AND for paging
- aggregate out route_group
- add hold-down + maybe min traffic
Can someone own a PR to the alert bundle? (observability-pack)

Sergio Costa: I can take it. will update rules in `observability-pack/pr-907` follow-up. target today.

Allison Grant: please include before/after replay using yesterday’s data if you can (use the simulate endpoint recordings?). we should verify we still would have paged on the jan 15 real impairment.

Miles Thornton: +1. for failover automation, we care about fast confirmable signals. slow burn is for “things are bad, look soon” not wakeups.

Aisha Bello: suggested mapping:
- fast AND (multiwindow) => PagerDuty P1
- slow only => Slack #oncall-sre + Jira ticket
- annotations include direct link to “region health evaluation logs” (PR-19835?)

Allison Grant: love that. also add runbook link in annotations: `hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook`.

Sergio Costa: ack. Draft rule shape:
```
# page only if fast + slow
(BR_5m > 14) and (BR_30m > 6)
# aggregate by region, scope
sum without(route_group, model, tenant) (...)
# min traffic
and (requests_30m > 5000)
```
Plus keep reachability separate.

Rafael Mendes: `requests_30m > 5000` seems high for some smaller regions at night. can we do per-region baseline? or just lower?

Aisha Bello: maybe `> 1000` and we can tune. also we can make it “or dedicated_pool_traffic > 200” since dedicated has lower rps but high impact.

Miles Thornton: keep it simple: start with 1000 and watch.

Allison Grant: +1. and add a note that if traffic is low, we rely more on reachability + latency + control plane health.

PagerDuty: Resolved: [P1] multi_region_availability_burn_rate_fast (service=hosted-api, region=eu-west)

Tomáš Novák: lol resolved while we’re here.

Sergio Costa: ok I’ll ship changes w/ feature flag in alert bundle so we can roll out without surprises.

Rafael Mendes: cool. pls post diff + new thresholds here before merge.

title: #eng-sre: 1768526344
preview:
Rafael Mendes: We need to settle anti-flap + dedupe for perf-canary alerts before we turn on paging. Current incident-bot handler is basically “one Slack thread per firing alertname”, which is… not enough.

Allison Grant: +1. We’re going to get spam if p95 oscillates around threshold. Need: (1) alert rule dampening, (2) incident-bot dedupe key, (3) clear “resolved” behavior.

Aisha Bello: For alerting side: what are we thinking for minimum samples? If the canary is low QPS for overhead reasons, we can’t do 1-min windows.

Nadia Rahman: FWIW telemetry pipeline produces stable histograms at 1m resolution but variance is high under 50 req/min, especially for p95/p99. I’d strongly prefer the evaluator enforce a min sample size gate.

Sean Gallagher: From on-call perspective: fewer pages > faster pages. I’m ok delaying page by 10–15m if it reduces false positives.

Logan Wright: Dedupe key proposal: incident-bot should dedupe on a normalized “incident identity” that doesn’t include the volatile labels (baseline hash, run_id, pod). Something like:

Logan Wright: 
```
dedupe_key = (
  service="perf-canary",
  metric={latency_p95|tps_drop|queueing_p95},
  model,
  region,
  tier,
  severity
)
```

Logan Wright: optionally include hardware_sku but only if we *actually* route by SKU.

Allison Grant: Please don’t include runtime_build_id in dedupe key. That’ll explode threads during a rollout where we see both builds.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. But we still need the alert payload to show runtime_build_id + kernel tag for triage.

Caleb Johnson: +1 payload fields are fine. For dedupe, you want “same customer impact surface”. That’s model+region+tier+metric.

Aisha Bello: On anti-flap: can we do “X out of Y evaluations” gating? Example: p95 regression condition true for 3 out of last 5 minutes, AND min_samples >= N.

Nadia Rahman: Yes, evaluator already has per-window sample counts. Suggest min_samples per evaluation window AND across the combined dampening window.

Sean Gallagher: What numbers? If we set min_samples too high, we’ll never alert on lower-traffic regions.

Mateo Alvarez: For phase 1 (us-east), canary QPS should be enough. We can tune region-by-region later. But we need sane defaults now.

Allison Grant: Proposed default for warning:
- window: 10m rolling
- condition: regression >= threshold for 2 consecutive evals (each eval 5m)
- min_samples: >= 200 requests total over 10m for that model/region/tier
- hold-down: once fired, don’t re-fire for 30m unless severity escalates

Allison Grant: Paging:
- window: 15m rolling
- condition: 3 consecutive evals (5m each)
- min_samples: >= 400 requests total
- hold-down: 60m

Rafael Mendes: That hold-down is for *new* incidents right? We still want updates posted in the existing thread.

Caleb Johnson: incident-bot can do: if dedupe_key exists and status=firing, post “still firing” every 15m max (rate-limit updates). And on resolved, post a resolution note.

Logan Wright: We should also set a max update frequency per dedupe_key. E.g. no more than 1 message / 10m per incident, unless severity changes.

Aisha Bello: Severity change handling: warning -> page should post in same thread but also page the on-call channel. Dedupe key includes severity right now, which would create a new thread.

Allison Grant: Good catch. Dedupe should not include severity then. Severity should be a field that can change within the same incident.

Logan Wright: Ok updated:

Logan Wright: 
```
dedupe_key = (
  service="perf-canary",
  metric,
  model,
  region,
  tier
)

thread_action:
- if new dedupe_key: create thread
- if existing: update thread
- if severity escalates: @pager + add "ESCALATED" line, keep same thread
```

Sean Gallagher: That works for me. One thread = one “thing broken”. If it gets worse, same thread.

Nadia Rahman: On min_samples: define “request” consistently. A canary run can be streaming; do we count per request or per token?

Mateo Alvarez: For regression definition we’re using request-level histograms (latency per request) and aggregate tokens/sec. So min_samples should be request count for latency metrics. For tokens/sec, maybe min_samples is “total generated tokens” to reduce noise.

Aisha Bello: +1. tokens/sec can swing with small token totals.

Allison Grant: Suggest:
- latency metrics: min_requests >= 200 (warn), >= 400 (page)
- tokens/sec metric: min_tokens >= 200k (warn), >= 400k (page)

Nadia Rahman: 200k tokens in 10m might be high for some models if we’re limiting overhead.

Rafael Mendes: Let’s not block on perfect numbers. Can we set tokens/sec min gate as “min_requests >= 100 AND min_tokens >= 50k” for warn, then revisit?

Sean Gallagher: Works. And we can disable paging for tokens/sec initially, only warn.

Allison Grant: Yes: page only on p95 latency regressions for top models in phase 1. tokens/sec stays warn-only until we have a week of data.

Caleb Johnson: Dedupe edge case: region outage causes canary timeouts, we might fire both “latency_p95 regression” and “canary_error_rate high”. Those should be separate dedupe_keys, but same root cause.

Rafael Mendes: That’s fine. On-call can correlate. Also, canary_error_rate should route to SRE but not page runtime.

Allison Grant: Routing matrix reminder:
- perf regression (latency/tps): #eng-runtime + oncall-runtime when paging
- canary health (errors/timeouts): #eng-sre first

incident-bot: (system) Noted: update planned for dedupe_key to exclude severity; rate-limit updates; resolution message enabled. (reactions: :white_check_mark:)

Logan Wright: I’ll update incident-bot handler accordingly. Need final list of fields in payload + link templates (Grafana dashboard + runbook).

Aisha Bello: I’ll update alert rule drafts: add min sample gate, X-of-Y evaluations, hold-down, and warn-only for tokens/sec. Will post PR for review.

Sean Gallagher: Please include in the bot message a single-line summary:
“model=…, region=…, tier=…, metric=…, observed=…, baseline=…, delta=…, samples=…, runtime_build=…, kernel=…”
The first message should be enough to decide if it’s real.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also add “next steps” line with runbook link and who to page if kernel tag changed.

Allison Grant: Cool. Action items:
- Logan: incident-bot dedupe + update throttling + severity escalation in same thread
- Aisha: alert policy updates (min samples, X-of-Y, warn/page split)
- Nadia: sanity-check sample gates vs expected canary QPS (us-east)

Mateo Alvarez: Thanks. Let’s keep it conservative. I’d rather miss one mild regression than page the world for noise.

title: #eng-sre: 1768479010
preview:
Rafael Mendes: need to lock acceptance criteria for failover controller dry-run this week. what *exactly* must it output so we can call ENG-2415 “done”? drop bullets here.

Miles Thornton: +1. baseline: dry-run should behave like prod decisioning but with zero writes. Output needs to be enough for on-call to say “yes execute” or “nope false positive”.

Jordan Lee: from policy engine side: MUST be deterministic given the same signal snapshot + config (no nondeterministic ordering). also include the “decision trace” so we can reproduce.

Mina Haddad: pls include policy diff output. like “route chat/completions tier=hosted: us-east weight 100→0, eu-west 0→100” and any fallbacks invoked (variant/model) if applicable.

Benji Okafor: and scope. “applies_to”: tenant tier / allowlist / max blast radius gates that would be enforced if executed. dry-run should show what it *would* block too.

Allison Grant: observability asks:
- emit a metric for dry-run evaluations + outcome (no-op / recommend_failover / recommend_rollback)
- include evidence signals with current values + thresholds (reachability, 5xx burn, p99 burn, capacity headroom)
- timestamps + TTL on signal freshness. biggest pain is “is this stale?”

Sergio Costa: +1 on freshness. also: can we standardize output JSON for the CLI and for incident-bot to post? otherwise we’ll end up with 3 formats.

Devon Price: for incident-bot: minimum fields to post a summary:
- region
- recommended_action (failover/rollback/hold)
- confidence
- top_evidence (2–3 lines)
- dashboard links (region health, burn alerts, routing flips)

Rafael Mendes: what do we mean by “confidence”? we need something implementable, not hand-wavy.

Miles Thornton: propose a simple score 0–100 based on corroboration + signal priority. ex:
- reachability fail from perf-canary + control-plane probe = high
- only tail latency burn with no 5xx + normal reachability = low/medium
also include “reason_code” + “corroboration” list.

Jordan Lee: +1. keep confidence derived from explicit rule outcomes, not ML. e.g. `confidence_basis`: ["reachability_authoritative", "5xx_fastburn_confirmed"]

Benji Okafor: and dry-run must show anti-flap / hold-down implications:
- whether action is suppressed by hold-down timer
- time remaining
- last flip time

Mina Haddad: also show capacity gating outcome. we had staging false positives where us-east looked bad but eu-west had no headroom, so the “recommended action” should be “hold + shed” not “failover”.

Allison Grant: yes. output should include “target_region_viable”: true/false and why (headroom %, queue depth, GPU pool saturation, etc). even if headroom signal is coarse.

Rafael Mendes: do we need a “predicted impact” section? like estimated error reduction / latency change?

Miles Thornton: nice-to-have, not required for v1. but we should at least show the *intended* mitigations (failover vs load-shed vs circuit break adjustments).

Devon Price: can we require an “audit event” even for dry-run? not in the compliance sense, but so we can reconstruct decisions in incident timeline.

Jordan Lee: audit event ok as long as it’s clearly `mode=dry_run` and no policy write.

Sergio Costa: re format, suggest we standardize on one blob (JSON) and CLI just pretty-prints. incident-bot can render the same fields.

Rafael Mendes: ok summarizing acceptance criteria draft — pls react :thumbsup: / edits.

Rafael Mendes: **Dry-run mode acceptance criteria (v1)**
1) **No side effects**: does not write routing policy, does not trigger pool drain, no config changes. Only reads signals/config.
2) **Deterministic**: same signal snapshot + config => same output; includes `decision_trace_id` / trace for repro.
3) **Recommended action**: outputs `recommended_action` in {`FAILOVER`, `ROLLBACK`, `HOLD`, `SHED_ONLY`, `NOOP`}.
4) **Policy diff**: for FAILOVER/ROLLBACK/SHED_ONLY, outputs proposed policy changes as a human-readable diff + machine JSON (routes/regions/weights).
5) **Scope + safety gates**: includes tenant tier/allowlist context and whether any safety limits would block execution (max flips/hr, blast radius, approvals required).
6) **Confidence + reason codes**: outputs numeric `confidence` (0–100) + `reason_codes` + `confidence_basis` derived from rule/corroboration (no ML).
7) **Evidence signals**: outputs top contributing signals with current value, threshold, window, and **freshness** (signal timestamp + age/TTL).
8) **Anti-flap visibility**: indicates hold-down / dampening state and time remaining, plus last action timestamp.
9) **Target viability**: for failover recommendations, includes `target_region_viable` and key gating facts (capacity headroom, reachability to target).
10) **Telemetry**: emits metric + structured log/audit event with `mode=dry_run`, `recommended_action`, `region`, `reason_codes`, and links/ids for dashboard correlation.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: looks solid. only tweak: include `signal_snapshot_id` so we can replay in simulate endpoint later.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: +1 `signal_snapshot_id`. also ensure trace contains ordered list of evaluated predicates (so we can see which one “won”).

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: add explicit line: dry-run must report when it *would* require approval hook in prod (even though it can’t execute).

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: on telemetry. for evidence signals, pls cap cardinality (don’t include tenant_id in labels). content in log is fine.

Sergio Costa: :thumbsup: +1 cap. also the diff should include “no-op” reason if it outputs HOLD/NOOP (eg hold-down active vs insufficient corroboration).

Devon Price: :thumbsup: works for incident-bot summary. i’ll align bot formatting to these fields once ENG-2415 lands.

Rafael Mendes: cool. I’ll paste this into ENG-2415 + link it from runbook. Miles/Jordan can you make sure `signal_snapshot_id` is feasible in the implementation? (even if it’s just a hash of inputs for now)

Miles Thornton: yep, we can do a stable hash of {signals+timestamps+config_version} as v1 and store full snapshot later.

Jordan Lee: works. if config_version is ambiguous, use policy bundle id (git SHA) so we can reproduce.

Rafael Mendes: done. thanks all.

Benji Okafor: and scope. “applies_to”: tenant tier / allowlist / max blast radius gates that would be enforced if executed. dry-run should show what it *would* block too.

- emit a metric for dry-run evaluations + outcome (no-op / recommend_failover / recommend_rollback)
- include evidence signals with current values + thresholds (reachability, 5xx burn, p99 burn, capacity headroom)
- timestamps + TTL on signal freshness. biggest pain is “is this stale?”

Sergio Costa: +1 on freshness. also: can we standardize output JSON for the CLI and for incident-bot to post? otherwise we’ll end up with 3 formats.

- confidence

title: #eng-sre: 1768476201
preview:
Rafael Mendes: heading into prod game day next week — can we align on *must-have* dashboards + alert tuning? I don’t want us staring at 12 tabs and missing the one signal that matters. What’s the minimum set + what annotations do we need in place before go/no-go?

Allison Grant: +1. For game day we need a single “Failover Game Day” folder (Grafana) with panels in the order we’ll use them.
Must-have (IMO):
1) Region health aggregate (overall + per-signal)
2) Cross-region reachability probes (perf-canary) w/ p50/p95 + failure rate
3) API 5xx burn rate (multiwindow) split by region + route group
4) Tail latency burn rate (p99) split by region + route group
5) Capacity headroom + queue depth per region (hosted + dedicated)
6) Policy engine decisions: recommended vs executed flips, reason + confidence
7) Gateway retry/timeout counters (to catch thundering herd)
8) Dedicated drain progress if we do any pool drain steps

Sergio Costa: re: “single folder” — yes. I can pull together a dashboard with those panels, but we need to agree on the dimensions or it’s going to explode.
Also: do we want panels by `tier` (hosted free/pro/enterprise, dedicated) or keep it simpler for game day?

Aisha Bello: for *game day* I’d keep it to `tier in (hosted_prod, dedicated_prod)` and *no tenant labels*.
We can add a drilldown dashboard later. Otherwise cardinality + slower queries when everything’s already on fire.

Miles Thornton: + keep the “region health aggregate” panel as the *top* panel. That’s the entry point for “is this real impairment or just one service”.
Also want a panel showing “corroboration signals present” (reachability + 5xx + latency + capacity) since ADR-042 requires corroboration for automation.

Allison Grant: yep. We can model that as a table panel:
- signal
- current state (OK/WARN/FAIL)
- time since state change
- source (probe / SLO burn / capacity)

Victor Alvarez: networking asks: we need *some* view into DNS + anycast symptoms. Even if it’s just:
- resolver success rate (internal)
- upstream provider status ping (best effort)
- connection error codes to edge
In past events “reachability fail” was actually resolver weirdness.

Rafael Mendes: agree. But keep it lightweight. If it becomes a “network deep dive” it won’t get used.

Sergio Costa: ok, I’ll add a “Reachability + DNS” row:
- perf-canary probe success rate per region pair (us-east→eu-west etc)
- edge connect errors (SYN timeout / TLS handshake)
- dns resolution time + NXDOMAIN rate (internal resolver)

Devon Price: on annotations: we *need* automation-driven annotations or the timeline is going to be a mess.
Proposal:
- Every failover controller action emits an annotation event (recommend / execute / rollback)
- Manual operator actions via CLI also emit annotations (even in dry-run)
- Incident-bot posts a summary in-thread + link to dashboard with the time range pre-set

Tomáš Novák: +1 and please tie annotation names to runbook steps. If runbook says “Step 4: enable failover flag”, the annotation should literally say `RUNBOOK_STEP_4 enable flag` or similar.
(Otherwise people will write “did the thing” and post no details)

Allison Grant: Devon — do we already have audit events in the pipeline? If not, Grafana annotations won’t happen automatically.

Devon Price: we have the new failover audit event schema landing this week (from routing). I can wire a small exporter -> Loki annotations feed.
Worst case we use manual annotations (Grafana API) from incident-bot.

Aisha Bello: alert tuning: for game day specifically, we should:
- route pages to *game day oncall* only (no broad paging)
- set burn alerts to `notify` but not `page` unless we exceed rollback criteria
- add annotation links to “what does this alert mean” + the exact query

Miles Thornton: what are rollback criteria again for the drill? I want those *on* the dashboard.

Rafael Mendes: draft was:
- sustained 5xx > 2% for 3 min for top 3 routes *or*
- p99 latency > 2x baseline for 5 min *or*
- automation flaps more than 2 times in 15 min
+ anything that looks like customer-visible sustained errors beyond the blast radius.

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a “Game Day Guardrails” text panel at top with those thresholds + links to the runbook section.

Vanessa Ortiz: capacity panels: I can provide the headroom metric but define “headroom” carefully.
We should chart:
- `gpu_tokens_per_s_capacity` vs `gpu_tokens_per_s_demand`
- queue depth / pending requests
- autoscaler saturation (desired vs current)
And include a big red indicator if we’re <15% headroom in the target failover region.

Allison Grant: for hosted, also add cache hit rate (prefix/KV) if available — during failover it can tank and cost/latency go sideways.
But ok if that’s a v2.

Victor Alvarez: note: reachability probes should show *directionality*. us-east→eu-west != eu-west→us-east depending on egress path.

Sergio Costa: yep. Panel will show both directions.

Tomáš Novák: can we also add “paging noise” view? During rehearsal we got spammed by unrelated alerts.
A panel listing firing alerts in the last 30m + whether they’re tagged `failover_signal=true`.

Aisha Bello: I can tag the alert rules and create a filtered view. Also we should temporarily silence non-failover pages during the drill window (with an explicit exception list).

Rafael Mendes: sounds good. Action items:
- @Sergio Costa build the Grafana folder/dashboard with agreed rows + guardrails text panel
- @Devon Price wire annotations from controller/audit events + manual CLI hook fallback
- @Aisha Bello tune burn alerts for game day routing + tags + “firing alerts” panel
- @Vanessa Ortiz publish capacity headroom queries + thresholds
- @Victor Alvarez provide minimal DNS/connectivity metrics + queries
Deadline: EOD Friday for go/no-go review.

Miles Thornton: please also add a “policy flips count” graph (per 5m) — anti-flap should be obvious visually.

Sergio Costa: yep.
Also question: do we want a separate dashboard for Dedicated drain progress? Or include a collapsible row?

Rafael Mendes: include a collapsible row for now. If we expand scope later we can split.

Allison Grant: +1. Keep the primary dashboard usable for Hosted-only too.

Devon Price: for incident-bot, I’ll include:
- current region health summary
- last 3 automation decisions with reasons
- links: dashboard (time range `now-1h`), region health logs, runbook

Aisha Bello: one more: can we add an explicit panel for “SLO classification: regional vs partial degradation”? We kept arguing about that in rehearsal.
Even if it’s a simple counter of `region_event=true` vs `service_event=true` based on the aggregator output.

Miles Thornton: yes please. That directly impacts whether automation is even allowed.

Sergio Costa: ok, adding.
I’ll post a draft link here once it’s up.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: thanks all. Let’s treat this as a go/no-go requirement — no dashboard, no prod test.

Sergio Costa: draft dashboard link (WIP): https://grafana.redwoodinference.net/d/failover-gameday/failover-game-day?orgId=1
Missing still: DNS panel (waiting on Victor), annotations (waiting on Devon).

Victor Alvarez: will drop queries in this thread in ~1h. Need to sanitize label set first.

Devon Price: annotations exporter is in progress. If I can’t land it today, I’ll at least give you a grafana-annotations API script we can run from incident-bot.

Tomáš Novák: once this is in place I’ll update the runbook with “which panels to look at in what order” + screenshots.

Rafael Mendes: perfect. Keep updates in-thread.
