title: #eng-sre: 1768464022
preview:
Allison Grant: seeing some noisy trigger signals in staging today. region-health is flipping `us-east` between degraded/ok every few mins, and failover-controller dry-run is recommending failover ~1x/10m 😬

Allison Grant: dashboard link (staging): https://grafana.staging.redwood/p/d/failover-signals?var=region=us-east

Miles Thornton: which signal is causing the downgrade? reachability probe or 5xx burn?

Allison Grant: looks like reachability is intermittently failing from eu-west->us-east (perf-canary). 5xx burn is basically flat.

Victor Alvarez: +1 I saw a few probe timeouts. might be our NAT gateway in staging, not the region itself. the TCP handshake is fine but the HTTP probe sometimes stalls.

Sergio Costa: if reachability is treated as authoritative (ADR-042 direction), this will create false positives unless we dampen / corroborate.

Miles Thornton: agreed reachability should be high priority for real region partitions, but we can’t let a flaky synthetic take down policy.

Rafael Mendes: reminder: for prod we said “reachability > 5xx burn > tail latency burn > capacity” but with required corroboration for non-catastrophic. staging should match the logic.

Jordan Lee: current policy-engine eval (as of pr-19812) treats `reachability=fail` as immediate `region=unhealthy` after 2 consecutive samples (30s interval). no corroboration gate yet.

Benji Okafor: that’s too aggressive for a single signal stream, esp synthetic. can we require 2-of-3 sources for reachability? (perf-canary + external ping mesh + control-plane heartbeat)

Victor Alvarez: we don’t have external mesh in staging today. but we can at least require perf-canary + control-plane heartbeat failure before declaring “hard down”.

Aisha Bello: from an SLO standpoint, we should distinguish “region unreachable” vs “service degraded” anyway. reachability should flip to `unreachable` only when it’s consistent.

Tomáš Novák: and on-call readability: right now the alert just says “REGION UNHEALTHY” w/o signal evidence. can we include the trigger evidence in alert annotations?

Sergio Costa: yep. I can add alert annotations for top contributing signal + last N samples. (we already emit `trigger_source` in staging).

Miles Thornton: proposal:
1) add dampening for reachability: require fail >90s (or 4/5 samples) before it’s eligible
2) require corroboration for automated actions: reachability fail alone can mark region “suspect” but not execute failover unless either 5xx burn OR latency burn corroborates, OR control-plane heartbeat also failing
3) still allow manual override from on-call

Ravi Menon: careful: if we require 5xx/latency corroboration, we’ll be late for pure partitions (clients can’t reach, so they don’t generate 5xx from the region). but we can corroborate with “ingress sees drop to near-zero” or “gateway health endpoints unreachable”.

Allison Grant: good call. we do have `edge_ingress_requests` by region. in a partition, traffic to that region falls off. we can use that as corroboration for reachability.

Jordan Lee: we can implement as: reachability fail + (edge_ingress_requests_rate < floor) => declare unreachable. else remain suspect.

Vanessa Ortiz: also capacity signal is noisy in staging because autoscaler is constantly resizing. we should avoid treating `capacity_exhaustion=true` unless sustained and accompanied by queueing/backpressure.

Miles Thornton: agree, capacity should never single-handedly trip regional failover. it should only steer within-region or shed load.

Benji Okafor: can we add a hold-down timer so once we mark suspect we don’t immediately go back to ok and back again? anti-flap matters even in dry-run.

Jordan Lee: there’s a `min_state_duration` config knob, default 2m but it’s only applied after a state change. in this case we’re bouncing because reachability flips before state commits.

Sergio Costa: metrics view: reachability sample is spiking failure ~10% (random). with 2 consecutive samples required, it’s not rare.

Victor Alvarez: I can try to fix the probe itself (timeout, keepalive, less reliance on that NAT path). current timeout is 500ms which is tight.

Allison Grant: 500ms is wild. even healthy cross-region in staging can exceed that.

Victor Alvarez: yeah. I’ll bump to 2s and add retries with jitter, and publish both “hard fail” vs “slow” as separate series.

Aisha Bello: +1 split. treat “slow” as latency burn input not reachability.

Miles Thornton: ok concrete next steps:
- Victor: update perf-canary probe timeouts + expose slow vs fail
- Jordan/Ravi: update policy eval: reachability requires 4/5 samples over 2m AND corroboration with `edge_ingress_requests_rate` floor OR control-plane heartbeat
- Sergio/Allison: update alert annotations to include evidence + suppress paging in staging until signal stabilizes

Tomáš Novák: can we capture the decision in the staging runbook too? on-call folks keep asking why it says “failover recommended”.

Rafael Mendes: I’ll add a note to the failover-controller runbook draft: reachability is “suspect” until corroborated, and dry-run output should show confidence + evidence.

Allison Grant: speaking of dry-run: it currently outputs `confidence=0.9` even when it’s just reachability. we should tie confidence to corroboration count.

Jordan Lee: fair. I can adjust confidence scoring: base +0.3 per corroborating class (reachability/error/latency/capacity/control-plane), cap at 1.0.

Ravi Menon: and make sure we don’t explode metric cardinality when adding “evidence” fields. keep evidence in logs/audit events, not in labels.

Sergio Costa: +1. metrics should keep `failover_reason` coarse. evidence goes to audit log (pr-19824).

Miles Thornton: cool. let’s aim to land the dampening + corroboration logic before next rehearsal. we can tolerate staging noise but not build muscle memory around bad recs.

Allison Grant: I’ll open a quick tracking issue in ENG-2412 comments + paste this thread summary.

Victor Alvarez: ack, I’ll push probe changes EOD. will post graphs after.

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: also suggest: per-tenant safety limits should ignore these suspect flips entirely (don’t count toward max flips/hour). otherwise we’ll self-throttle during real events.

Jordan Lee: good point. I’ll make sure only executed flips increment the limiter.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. keep the signal hierarchy sharp but practical. false positives are the #1 way we lose trust in automation.
text:
Allison Grant: seeing some noisy trigger signals in staging today. region-health is flipping `us-east` between degraded/ok every few mins, and failover-controller dry-run is recommending failover ~1x/10m 😬

Allison Grant: dashboard link (staging): https://grafana.staging.redwood/p/d/failover-signals?var=region=us-east

Miles Thornton: which signal is causing the downgrade? reachability probe or 5xx burn?

Allison Grant: looks like reachability is intermittently failing from eu-west->us-east (perf-canary). 5xx burn is basically flat.

Victor Alvarez: +1 I saw a few probe timeouts. might be our NAT gateway in staging, not the region itself. the TCP handshake is fine but the HTTP probe sometimes stalls.

Sergio Costa: if reachability is treated as authoritative (ADR-042 direction), this will create false positives unless we dampen / corroborate.

Miles Thornton: agreed reachability should be high priority for real region partitions, but we can’t let a flaky synthetic take down policy.

Rafael Mendes: reminder: for prod we said “reachability > 5xx burn > tail latency burn > capacity” but with required corroboration for non-catastrophic. staging should match the logic.

Jordan Lee: current policy-engine eval (as of pr-19812) treats `reachability=fail` as immediate `region=unhealthy` after 2 consecutive samples (30s interval). no corroboration gate yet.

Benji Okafor: that’s too aggressive for a single signal stream, esp synthetic. can we require 2-of-3 sources for reachability? (perf-canary + external ping mesh + control-plane heartbeat)

Victor Alvarez: we don’t have external mesh in staging today. but we can at least require perf-canary + control-plane heartbeat failure before declaring “hard down”.

Aisha Bello: from an SLO standpoint, we should distinguish “region unreachable” vs “service degraded” anyway. reachability should flip to `unreachable` only when it’s consistent.

Tomáš Novák: and on-call readability: right now the alert just says “REGION UNHEALTHY” w/o signal evidence. can we include the trigger evidence in alert annotations?

Sergio Costa: yep. I can add alert annotations for top contributing signal + last N samples. (we already emit `trigger_source` in staging).

Miles Thornton: proposal:
1) add dampening for reachability: require fail >90s (or 4/5 samples) before it’s eligible
2) require corroboration for automated actions: reachability fail alone can mark region “suspect” but not execute failover unless either 5xx burn OR latency burn corroborates, OR control-plane heartbeat also failing
3) still allow manual override from on-call

Ravi Menon: careful: if we require 5xx/latency corroboration, we’ll be late for pure partitions (clients can’t reach, so they don’t generate 5xx from the region). but we can corroborate with “ingress sees drop to near-zero” or “gateway health endpoints unreachable”.

Allison Grant: good call. we do have `edge_ingress_requests` by region. in a partition, traffic to that region falls off. we can use that as corroboration for reachability.

Jordan Lee: we can implement as: reachability fail + (edge_ingress_requests_rate < floor) => declare unreachable. else remain suspect.

Vanessa Ortiz: also capacity signal is noisy in staging because autoscaler is constantly resizing. we should avoid treating `capacity_exhaustion=true` unless sustained and accompanied by queueing/backpressure.

Miles Thornton: agree, capacity should never single-handedly trip regional failover. it should only steer within-region or shed load.

Benji Okafor: can we add a hold-down timer so once we mark suspect we don’t immediately go back to ok and back again? anti-flap matters even in dry-run.

Jordan Lee: there’s a `min_state_duration` config knob, default 2m but it’s only applied after a state change. in this case we’re bouncing because reachability flips before state commits.

Sergio Costa: metrics view: reachability sample is spiking failure ~10% (random). with 2 consecutive samples required, it’s not rare.

Victor Alvarez: I can try to fix the probe itself (timeout, keepalive, less reliance on that NAT path). current timeout is 500ms which is tight.

Allison Grant: 500ms is wild. even healthy cross-region in staging can exceed that.

Victor Alvarez: yeah. I’ll bump to 2s and add retries with jitter, and publish both “hard fail” vs “slow” as separate series.

Aisha Bello: +1 split. treat “slow” as latency burn input not reachability.

Miles Thornton: ok concrete next steps:
- Victor: update perf-canary probe timeouts + expose slow vs fail
- Jordan/Ravi: update policy eval: reachability requires 4/5 samples over 2m AND corroboration with `edge_ingress_requests_rate` floor OR control-plane heartbeat
- Sergio/Allison: update alert annotations to include evidence + suppress paging in staging until signal stabilizes

Tomáš Novák: can we capture the decision in the staging runbook too? on-call folks keep asking why it says “failover recommended”.

Rafael Mendes: I’ll add a note to the failover-controller runbook draft: reachability is “suspect” until corroborated, and dry-run output should show confidence + evidence.

Allison Grant: speaking of dry-run: it currently outputs `confidence=0.9` even when it’s just reachability. we should tie confidence to corroboration count.

Jordan Lee: fair. I can adjust confidence scoring: base +0.3 per corroborating class (reachability/error/latency/capacity/control-plane), cap at 1.0.

Ravi Menon: and make sure we don’t explode metric cardinality when adding “evidence” fields. keep evidence in logs/audit events, not in labels.

Sergio Costa: +1. metrics should keep `failover_reason` coarse. evidence goes to audit log (pr-19824).

Miles Thornton: cool. let’s aim to land the dampening + corroboration logic before next rehearsal. we can tolerate staging noise but not build muscle memory around bad recs.

Allison Grant: I’ll open a quick tracking issue in ENG-2412 comments + paste this thread summary.

Victor Alvarez: ack, I’ll push probe changes EOD. will post graphs after.

Benji Okafor: :thumbsup: also suggest: per-tenant safety limits should ignore these suspect flips entirely (don’t count toward max flips/hour). otherwise we’ll self-throttle during real events.

Jordan Lee: good point. I’ll make sure only executed flips increment the limiter.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. keep the signal hierarchy sharp but practical. false positives are the #1 way we lose trust in automation.

title: #eng-sre: 1768461205
preview:
Talia Benson: Kicking off plan review for Q1 controlled failover game day (us-east -> eu-west). Need owners + go/no-go by EOD tomorrow so we can send exec update. Draft plan is in Confluence: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Controlled+Failover+Test+Plan+Q1+2026

Talia Benson: Proposed window: Wed 01/15 10:00–11:00 PT (30m active, 30m observe/rollback). Please confirm availability + owners for each step.

Sean Gallagher: Thx. Aiming for “controlled, measurable, reversible”. Let’s align on success criteria + hard rollback triggers in this thread.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also want a very crisp checklist for go/no-go. We got burned last rehearsal by “we assumed X was on”.

Miles Thornton: I skimmed the plan. Overall looks good. A few big questions:
1) What’s our authoritative trigger for declaring “region impaired” during the test (since we’re inducing it)?
2) Are we validating automation path (controller) vs manual policy flip?
3) What are the “don’t fail over” scenarios in case signals get noisy?

Allison Grant: For observability: do we agree on a single dashboard URL for the war room? I can pin the panels + add annotations from the controller if we have the event stream wired.

Sergio Costa: I can update the dashboard pack today. Current draft has: reachability probes, 5xx burn, p95/p99 latency burn, capacity headroom, policy flips, gateway retry-after counts.

Aisha Bello: For SLO math, we should call out what we’re measuring: multi-region availability from edge POV (successful responses / total) and not internal service health. Success criteria should include “no sustained 2%+ error rate for >N minutes” or similar.

Talia Benson: Owners I have in the doc (pls confirm):
- Run-of-show / timekeeper: Talia
- Failover controller operator: Rafael
- Routing/policy support: Ravi + Mina
- Region health / signals: Miles
- Observability / dashboards: Allison + Sergio
- Network/DNS watch: Victor
- Capacity / headroom + drain readiness: Vanessa
- Gateway/circuit breaker watch: Peter
- Incident-bot/status comms: Devon

Ravi Menon: Confirmed for routing/policy support. We can run controller in “dry-run + approve” mode so it emits recommended actions before we execute.

Mina Haddad: Confirmed. Please make sure we scope routes/models for the test. I want it pinned to a specific route group + allowlist tenants only.

Benji Okafor: For safety limits: we can enforce max policy flips per tenant per hour, plus global rate limit on controller actions. If we’re doing approve mode, the “flip” is still one action but I want to validate the limiter counters are visible.

Jordan Lee: Policy engine note: deterministic evaluation is in place, but if any input signal is missing, we need a default. In rehearsal we had “missing signal = unknown” which caused conservative behavior. Decide now: unknown should NOT trigger failover.

Miles Thornton: +1. For this test, missing signal should be treated as “no corroboration” and block automation, not force it.

Emily Zhao: Reachability probes are green in staging; for prod we should make sure the perf-canary probes are running from at least 2 external vantage points. Otherwise we’ll confuse internal packet loss with actual ingress reachability.

Victor Alvarez: Agree. Also please call out in plan: DNS TTL change is already rolled out to 60s for api.redwoodinference.com, but some resolvers ignore. Don’t assume instant traffic steering. We should measure actual regional traffic shift via edge logs.

Vanessa Ortiz: Capacity: eu-west has ~35% headroom right now for hosted. Dedicated is tighter. If we include Dedicated, we need explicit “safe to failover” criteria per pool.

Irene Choi: Infra POV: I’m ok with hosted-only for first prod game day. Dedicated introduces too many variables unless we pre-drain and confirm spare GPUs. If we do Dedicated, do it as separate scenario.

Peter Holtz: Gateway: please include a rollback trigger on elevated retries/timeouts. If clients start hammering due to retries, we’ll see a thundering herd. I can watch `retry_after_emitted` and `upstream_timeout_rate`.

Devon Price: For comms: do we want incident-bot to post in #incidents or keep it in #eng-sre? For controlled test I suggest #eng-sre + a stub status-page note (planned maintenance-style) if we think customers might notice.

Sean Gallagher: For this one: no status-page post unless we actually impact customers beyond allowlisted tenants. But we should have copy ready. Devon, can you prep the template + approval path?

Devon Price: Yep. I’ll prep “planned reliability test” messaging and the “we observed transient errors due to a controlled test” fallback.

Tomáš Novák: Can we lock the runbook entry points? During rehearsal folks weren’t sure whether to start with Hosted API runbook vs routing controller runbook. Recommend we paste 3 links at top of the plan + assign one “single source of truth”.

Talia Benson: Good call. I’ll add a “Start here” section with:
1) Hosted API regional failover runbook
2) Routing policy failover controller runbook
3) GPU pool drain procedure (FYI)

Allison Grant: Re annotations: if controller emits audit events, I can scrape and add vertical markers to Grafana. If that’s not ready, we should at least have someone manually annotate times in Slack with “T+X action Y” so we can reconstruct.

Rafael Mendes: Controller audit events are in the latest build, but I’m not 100% it’s wired to Grafana annotations. Let’s plan for manual time-stamps as backup.

Sergio Costa: I can add a simple “annotation helper” panel that shows the audit stream entries; might be enough for the retro.

Miles Thornton: Let’s define success criteria clearly. Proposal:
- Controller detects induced impairment in <= 60s (from start of impairment injection)
- Traffic shift begins <= 120s
- 5xx rate stays < 1% overall; allowlisted tenants may see brief spike < 5% for <= 2 minutes
- p99 latency does not exceed 2x baseline for more than 5 minutes
- No more than 1 automated rollback unless explicitly invoked

Aisha Bello: On the SLO side, can we phrase it as:
- “No material error budget burn”: e.g., keep 30-day equivalent burn < 1% during the window (using fast-burn lens)
That’s easier to compare later.

Sean Gallagher: I like Miles’ thresholds, but we should add hard rollback triggers:
- sustained 5xx > 2% for 3 mins across non-allowlisted traffic (should be ~0)
- elevated timeouts + retry amplification (Peter’s metrics)
- eu-west capacity headroom < 15% for > 5 mins

Vanessa Ortiz: For capacity trigger, use “effective headroom” (accounts for reserved Dedicated) not raw GPU free. I’ll post the query we should watch.

Vanessa Ortiz: Query draft (Datadog): `avg(last_5m):redwood.fleet.headroom_effective{region:eu-west,service:hosted-api} < 0.15`

Victor Alvarez: Network rollback trigger: if cross-region reachability probes show >20% packet loss or >500ms RTT from 2+ vantage points, we should stop injecting and revert. Otherwise we’re testing “eu-west can’t be reached” which defeats the purpose.

Ravi Menon: Re: “impairment injection” — what exactly are we doing? Options:
A) Disable upstream in us-east for a route group (simulate 5xx)
B) Blackhole a % of traffic at gateway
C) Introduce latency via fault injection
Need to pick one that doesn’t confuse signals.

Miles Thornton: Plan currently says A: set routing to prefer us-east, then degrade us-east data plane by lowering pool capacity and increasing error rate via a controlled kill switch on one service shard. That will trip 5xx burn + reachability stays OK.

Peter Holtz: If reachability stays OK, great. That lets us validate hierarchy: 5xx burn should dominate over latency burn and capacity. Just make sure we don’t also trigger capacity exhaustion in eu-west at same time.

Mina Haddad: +1. Also want to verify the “hold-down timer” behavior: once we flip to eu-west, we should not flap back when us-east partially recovers during the test.

Jordan Lee: Hold-down currently 15 minutes default. For game day we might want to shorten to 5 minutes to observe rollback logic within the window, but that’s risky. Recommend keep 15m and do rollback manually after validation.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. We can do:
- Phase 1: induce us-east impairment -> fail over to eu-west
- Phase 2: stop impairment injection, keep traffic on eu-west for observation
- Phase 3: manual rollback at T+30m (or earlier if triggers hit)

Talia Benson: Open question: do we include streaming endpoints in allowlist? There’s a separate fix in flight for reconnect-on-redirect.

Ravi Menon: I’d include 1 streaming tenant in allowlist as a canary, but only if the reconnect fix is deployed. Otherwise we’ll generate noise + support tickets.

Allison Grant: If streaming is included, we need a metric panel for `stream_disconnects` and `reconnect_success_rate`. Otherwise we can’t tell if it’s client behavior or routing.

Devon Price: Support risk: if any non-allowlisted tenant sees errors, #support will get pings fast. Can we pre-brief Dev Patel / Owen Phillips with the window + what to say?

Sean Gallagher: Yes. Talia, can you add “Support heads-up” task? Keep it internal; do not proactively email customers for this one.

Talia Benson: Yep. Action items from this thread (I’ll roll into plan):
- Confirm hosted-only for game day 1 (Dedicated separate) (Irene/Vanessa)
- Lock allowlist tenants + route group + models (Mina/Ravi)
- Success criteria + rollback triggers (Sean/Miles/Aisha)
- Dashboard URL + annotation plan (Allison/Sergio)
- Reachability probe coverage validation (Emily/Victor)
- Support heads-up + comms templates (Devon/Talia)
- Runbook “Start here” section (Tomáš/Talia)

Irene Choi: Confirmed hosted-only for this run.

Vanessa Ortiz: Confirmed. I’ll attach the headroom snapshot for eu-west and “min headroom” thresholds in the doc.

Allison Grant: I’ll post the dashboard link here once updated. Also pls confirm if we want alerts temporarily muted for allowlisted tenants so we don’t page the world.

Tomáš Novák: Strong +1 on muting pages but keeping alerts visible. We can route them to #eng-sre instead of PagerDuty.

Sean Gallagher: That’s the right approach. Keep paging on for global signals; route tenant-scoped alerts to Slack during the window.

Devon Price: I’ll configure incident-bot to post a summary message on failover execute + rollback (who/what/why + links) in #eng-sre only.

Rafael Mendes: Can we do a quick 15-min “pre-flight” at 09:45 PT day-of to confirm:
- controller in approve mode
- allowlist configured
- dashboards open
- rollback authority present

Talia Benson: Yes. Adding pre-flight meeting + go/no-go checklist. I’ll post updated plan for final review tomorrow.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks all. Please react :thumbsup: on Talia’s updated plan once you’ve reviewed; no reaction = not approved.

Sergio Costa: I’ll have dashboard + alert routing ready by end of day. Will drop the link in #eng-sre.

Aisha Bello: I’ll draft the success criteria section w/ SLO framing and drop text for review.

Victor Alvarez: I’ll add a “DNS realities” note: TTL isn’t a promise; measure traffic shift via edge logs. Don’t over-index on resolver behavior.

Miles Thornton: I’ll update the plan to explicitly test signal hierarchy ordering and call out “unknown/missing signal blocks automation”.

Talia Benson: Great. I’ll follow up tomorrow with the consolidated doc + checklist.

Victor Alvarez: I’ll add a “DNS realities” note: TTL isn’t a promise; measure traffic shift via edge logs. Don’t over-index on resolver behavior.

Miles Thornton: I’ll update the plan to explicitly test signal hierarchy ordering and call out “unknown/missing signal blocks automation”.

Talia Benson: Great. I’ll follow up tomorrow with the consolidated doc + checklist.

title: #eng-sre: 1768438820
preview:
Aisha Bello: Kicking off severity policy for perf-canary alerts. Need agreement on: what’s WARN vs PAGE, and who gets paged when a top-model regresses. Goal is to avoid paging on noise but still catch real tail latency regressions fast.

Aisha Bello: Proposal v0:
- WARN: p95 e2e latency +8% vs baseline sustained 10m (min N=200) OR tokens/sec -8% sustained 10m (min N=200)
- PAGE (Sev2): p95 +15% sustained 15m (min N=500) OR tokens/sec -15% sustained 15m (min N=500)
- PAGE (Sev1): p95 +25% sustained 15m (min N=500) AND affects >=2 regions OR top-model in us-east

Aisha Bello: Routing:
- WARN -> #eng-runtime + #eng-sre (no page)
- PAGE -> oncall (SRE primary) + runtime secondary in thread

Sean Gallagher: +1 on tiered, but I’d like paging to be *only* for top-models initially. For non-top models, page is going to kill us during tuning.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. If we page for any model regression we’ll page weekly (at least) during kernel/scheduler iteration.

Allison Grant: Can we define “top model” concretely as a list in control plane config? If it’s implicit (top by usage) it’ll churn and change paging behavior unexpectedly.

Noah Patel: Yeah, canary config has a curated set anyway. We can tag models with `priority=top|high-risk|standard` and only enable paging for `top` in phase 1.

Aisha Bello: Works for me. We can have a constant allowlist for paging.

Mateo Alvarez: From runtime side: I’m fine with paging only top-models. But I want WARN to trigger for all models in canary so we see issues early without waking people.

Paula Mendes: Product request: paging should be strict and explainable. Also we need a clear “what happens” when paging triggers (e.g. pause rollout gates). But we can separate that from this thread.

Logan Wright: Metric question: are we using p95 end-to-end or runtime-only? p95 e2e includes queueing + gateway stuff; might be noisy.

Aisha Bello: I think severity policy should key on e2e p95 for customer impact, but include runtime-only + queueing breakdown in alert payload for triage.

Noah Patel: +1. Canary will emit both and we can put both in the payload.

Sean Gallagher: Thresholds: +8% warn seems ok *if* we have anti-flap. For paging, +15% might still be noisy depending on model variance. Can we require 2 consecutive windows / burn-rate style check?

Aisha Bello: Yes. Proposed anti-flap:
- Evaluate in 5m windows.
- WARN requires 2/2 windows breaching (10m total).
- PAGE requires 3/3 windows breaching (15m total).
- Clear requires 2/2 windows healthy.

Rafael Mendes: Please also add “cooldown” after a page fires so incident-bot doesn’t re-page every eval cycle.

Aisha Bello: Yep: 60m cooldown for paging alerts, dedupe key = model+region+tier+signal.

Allison Grant: Re: min sample sizes. Is N=500 realistic for low-QPS models? For top models yes, but others may never hit that.

Noah Patel: For non-top models we’re not paging; min sample can be lower for WARN. Also canary QPS is under a budget; N depends on QPS and window. We can do per-priority min sample.

Aisha Bello: Updated proposal:
- WARN (all canary models): +10% p95 e2e OR -10% tokens/sec, sustained 10m, min N=150
- PAGE Sev2 (top models only): +20% p95 e2e OR -20% tokens/sec, sustained 15m, min N=400
- PAGE Sev1 (top models only): +30% p95 e2e sustained 15m, min N=400, AND observed in >=2 regions OR also shows in runtime-only p95 (to avoid paging on pure gateway blips)

Sean Gallagher: That feels safer. I like adding a “runtime-only corroboration” for Sev1.

Logan Wright: For that corroboration, make sure runtime-only histogram has same labels; otherwise joining in alerting gets ugly.

Aisha Bello: Noted. We’ll keep the alert rule simple: for Sev1, require `runtime_p95` > baseline by +25% in same window (in addition to e2e).

Rafael Mendes: Who gets paged?
- SRE oncall always
- Runtime oncall as secondary only for Sev1? Or always add them for Sev2 too?

Mateo Alvarez: Prefer: Sev2 pages SRE only, but auto-notify #eng-runtime with context. Sev1 pages both SRE and runtime oncall.

Noah Patel: +1. Runtime folks will jump in on Sev2 anyway if it’s real, but no need to wake two people for a Sev2 that might be infra.

Allison Grant: Can we codify this in the policy doc + make sure the incident-bot message includes ownership hints? Like “looks like queueing-time increased -> infra/scheduler”.

Aisha Bello: Yes. Alert payload should include: baseline vs observed, confidence (sample size), and quick attribution fields: queueing delta, batch size shift, kernel tag change, runtime build tag.

Sean Gallagher: Also: paging hours? We page 24/7 for Sev1, but for Sev2 can we do business hours only during tuning?

Rafael Mendes: Strong preference: Sev2 paging 24/7 only after we’ve had 1-2 weeks of clean WARN-only. For phase 1, let’s keep Sev2 as “ticket + slack notify”, not a page.

Aisha Bello: Ok, so initial rollout:
- WARN enabled immediately (all canary models)
- PAGE Sev2 disabled (or routed to non-paging channel) for week 1
- PAGE Sev1 enabled for top models only once we’ve validated baseline + cardinality

Mateo Alvarez: Fine. But put a date or criteria for enabling Sev1 so it doesn’t drift.

Aisha Bello: Criteria:
- Baselines computed for top models in us-east
- 0 alert storms in 72h
- Telemetry ingestion latency <2m p95
- Oncall runbook reviewed

Allison Grant: Add “dashboard links verified” too. Otherwise it’s a page with no working links.

Aisha Bello: Added.

Aisha Bello: Action items:
- I’ll update the alert policy draft with: top-model allowlist, new thresholds, and routing matrix.
- @Rafael Mendes can you confirm paging target (PagerDuty service) + cooldown/dedupe requirements for incident-bot?
- @Noah Patel please confirm we can emit runtime-only p95 with same label set (model, model_version, region, tier, hardware_sku) w/ cardinality guardrails.

Rafael Mendes: Yep, will drop PD service + escalation chain in the doc. Cooldown 60m is good; dedupe key as you said.

Noah Patel: Will confirm with Logan/Omar. Label set should be stable; we’ll avoid per-request IDs. Hardware SKU might be optional for alerting but helpful for drilldown.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. Please keep the policy explicit: when in doubt, don’t page. We can always tighten once we trust the canary.

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: I’ll post updated draft in this thread by EOD.

title: #eng-sre: 1768148800
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Want to close the loop on “fail-open” + circuit breaker behavior for shadow traffic before we finalize alerts/runbook. I’m seeing slightly different assumptions between routing + telemetry + scoring.

Sean Gallagher: Requirements from SRE POV:
1) shadow path *cannot* add tail latency to user response (beyond noise)
2) shadow path failures must not reduce control path success rate
3) when shadow target or scoring/ingestion is unhealthy, we degrade by dropping shadow work (not retrying inline)

Mina Haddad: +1. On routing side we can make mirror fully async / detached from request lifecycle. For streaming we already decided: never wait on shadow chunks.

Ravi Menon: Same. Data plane will do “best effort enqueue” and then move on. But we need explicit timeout + breaker semantics so we’re not doing expensive work when the target is on fire.

Allison Grant: Observability angle: please don’t create a situation where high shadow error rate spams metrics/logs. Breaker should also reduce emission volume when things go bad.

Cole Summers: Also quota/usage: confirm shadow requests do NOT count against customer quota / rate limits? We had a partial implementation counting shadow tokens in one path.

Mina Haddad: Yeah, shadow should be billed/charged internally to Redwood (at least in beta). We’ll tag separately (`traffic_type=shadow`) and exclude from quota enforcement.

Sean Gallagher: Cool. Here’s what I’m proposing as the shared “contract”:
- Control request returns based on control target only.
- Shadow dispatch is fire-and-forget.
- No inline retries.
- Shadow dispatch has a hard time budget. If it can’t enqueue quickly, drop.
- Circuit breaker opens on (a) shadow target errors or (b) internal backpressure (queue lag / ingestion lag / CPU) and while open we skip mirroring entirely.

Vikram Singh: For (time budget) what’s “quickly”? We can do a tiny budget for enqueue to local buffer but remote call to shadow target can take longer.

Ravi Menon: Implementation detail: in the router we have to either (1) spawn shadow RPC in a separate executor and not await it OR (2) write shadow payload to a local queue and have a background worker do the RPC.

Mina Haddad: Current PR is (1) with strict timeouts, but we can make it “schedule onto shadow executor” and if the executor queue is full -> drop.

Sean Gallagher: I’d prefer we don’t do “RPC inline on the request thread”, even if we don’t await. Thread contention shows up as latency.

Mina Haddad: Agree. We can do: request thread does a cheap fingerprint + sampling decision + enqueue a small task onto shadow executor. If enqueue fails / is slow, drop.

Caleb Johnson: +1. Also telemetry emission should follow the same pattern. If we’re already behind, emit a minimal counter not a full event.

Allison Grant: Can we define what the circuit breaker watches?

Sean Gallagher: Proposed breaker signals (two tiers):
Tier A (fast, local):
- shadow executor queue depth > N (or enqueue latency > X ms) => open breaker
- local “shadow_work_drop_rate” high => open breaker
Tier B (remote target health):
- shadow target request error rate (>=500/timeout) > 5% over 1m AND sample_count >= 200 => open breaker
- shadow target p95 > 2s over 5m (or timeouts) => open breaker

Ravi Menon: Question: do we have “shadow target p95” without already doing the RPC? chicken/egg.

Allison Grant: We can measure it from completed shadow attempts. While breaker is open we’ll have fewer samples, but that’s ok.

Mina Haddad: Also if the target is slow but not erroring, we still don’t want to burn GPU/network. So opening on latency makes sense.

Sean Gallagher: Concrete thresholds (v1 beta) so we stop bikeshedding:
- Enqueue budget: 10ms p99 on request thread. If `enqueue_time > 10ms` => drop that shadow attempt.
- Shadow executor queue depth: if > 5,000 tasks OR enqueue failures > 1% over 1m => open breaker.
- Shadow target timeout: 2s hard timeout on shadow RPC.
- Breaker open conditions:
  a) shadow target error_rate > 5% over 1m (min 200 attempts)
  b) OR shadow target timeout_rate > 2% over 1m (min 200 attempts)
  c) OR shadow executor drop_rate > 10% over 1m (min 200 sampled)
- Breaker open duration: 5 minutes, then half-open with 1% sampling for 1 minute.

Cole Summers: 2s RPC timeout feels high if the target is the same infra. But since it’s async, the risk is cost not latency. I’m ok.

Ravi Menon: Works. For streaming shadowing: do we apply 2s per stream or per initial request?

Mina Haddad: For streaming: only mirror the initial request + capture first K tokens or first T seconds, whichever comes first. Shadow will have its own stop conditions.

Sean Gallagher: Put a pin in streaming specifics; breaker semantics should still apply. If we can’t schedule the streaming shadow worker quickly, we skip.

Allison Grant: On metrics emission: when breaker is open, can we avoid sending per-request shadow events? Just a counter like `shadow_skipped_total{reason=circuit_open}`.

Caleb Johnson: Yep. Also when drop happens due to backpressure, emit:
- `shadow_dispatch_dropped_total{reason=queue_full|enqueue_timeout|circuit_open}`
- and avoid logging per-request.

Nadia Rahman: Telemetry pipeline constraint: if we continue to emit shadow pair events while ingestion is lagging, we amplify the lag. Can breaker consume “ingestion lag” as a signal?

Sean Gallagher: Good call. Add Tier A signal:
- if `shadow_event_ingest_lag_seconds > 60` OR `shadow_scoring_queue_lag_seconds > 300` => open breaker.

Nadia Rahman: +1. I can expose those as gauges from pipeline.

Mina Haddad: One nuance: control-plane wants fail-open on policy fetch too. If policy service is down, we default to “no shadowing”, correct?

Sean Gallagher: Correct. Any config fetch/validation failure => shadow disabled. No 500s.

Ravi Menon: And if fingerprinting fails / missing fields, we just treat as not sampled.

Allison Grant: Paging policy: do we page on breaker open? That seems noisy.

Sean Gallagher: No page for breaker opening by itself. Page only if:
- breaker open for > 30m continuously (suggests sustained outage)
- OR control-path impact metrics move (p99 latency, error rate)
- OR cardinality / metrics stack health alerts
Breaker open should create a ticket + dashboard annotation.

Cole Summers: Also need a “big red switch” flag to disable shadowing per-tenant and globally.

Mina Haddad: Already in feature-flag plan: `shadow_traffic_enabled` (global) + `shadow_traffic_tenant_allowlist` for beta.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Action items:
- Mina/Ravi: update routing doc + implementation to enforce 10ms enqueue budget and skip mirroring when circuit open.
- Nadia/Caleb: expose ingest/scoring lag gauges that routing can read (or via shared health endpoint) + ensure event emission drops to counters while open.
- Allison: add dashboard panels for breaker state + drop reasons; no per-request logs.
- Sean: update runbook section “fail-open/circuit breaker thresholds” with the above.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: I’ll add a panel for `shadow_circuit_state{route,tenant}` but will bucket tenant for cardinality (Dedicated only gets tenant breakdown).

Nadia Rahman: :thumbsup: will post metric names in thread once wired.

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup: makes sense. I’ll align PR with these defaults; can keep thresholds configurable but set beta defaults as Sean listed.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks all. If anyone objects to the numbers, speak now; otherwise I’ll consider this “agreed” for beta.

Ravi Menon: No objections. Let’s ship and tune with real data.

Allison Grant: +1. We can always tighten once we see drop/lag patterns.

Sean Gallagher: Closing this out. Will link from the SLO doc + runbook.

Sean Gallagher: Closing this out. Will link from the SLO doc + runbook.

title: #eng-sre: 1768063011
preview:
Allison Grant: jumping in w/ quick cardinality risk review for shadow traffic dims before we bless ENG-5410 + dashboards. tl;dr: we need an explicit allowlist for *metrics* tags, everything else goes to events/logs only (sampled).

Sean Gallagher: +1. last time we shipped “just one more label” we paged ourselves for 2 days :)

Logan Wright: agreed. i pulled the draft schema list from the shadow event PR and the dashboard mockups. some dims are fine for events, but Prom/OTel metrics need hard constraints.

Nadia Rahman: can we clarify “metrics” vs “event payload”? we’re planning: (a) shadow_pair event (control+shadow) into analytics store, (b) pre-agg metrics emitted from ingestion/scoring.

Allison Grant: yep. this thread is about labels/dimensions on (b) pre-agg metrics (time series). event payload can be richer *if sampled* + retention is short.

Caleb Johnson: i’ll paste the current proposed metric tags from observability review doc:
- tenant_id
- route
- model_id_control
- model_id_shadow
- model_version_control
- model_version_shadow
- quant_profile
- region
- deployment_mode
- prompt_id
- request_fingerprint
- rubric_version
- metric_name
- outcome
- error_code

Sean Gallagher: :no_good: tenant_id + prompt_id + request_fingerprint are immediate “no” for metrics.

Marcus Lin: +1 from security. request_fingerprint in particular is quasi-identifier. even hashed, it becomes a join key and also explodes cardinality.

Ravi Menon: on routing side we need fingerprint for deterministic bucketing (control vs shadow consistency). but we don’t need to emit it as a metric label.

Logan Wright: exactly. fingerprint should live in event payload only, and even then: sample + TTL, and not queryable as a dimension in dashboards.

Allison Grant: ok, proposal: split dims into three buckets:
A) SAFE METRIC LABELS (bounded / low-card)
B) METRIC LABELS WITH GUARDRAILS (bucketed / capped)
C) EVENT-ONLY (never as metric label)

Selene Huang: and we should preserve enough segmentation to make quality deltas interpretable (by route/model/rubric + maybe customer tier). otherwise it’s “overall accuracy changed” and nobody trusts it.

Allison Grant: totally. for segmentation, we need labels that have an upper bound.

Logan Wright: suggested A) SAFE metric labels:
- deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated|private)
- region (bounded list)
- route_id (NOT route_name; internal id; enforce max per tenant)
- experiment_id (bounded-ish but still risky; see below)
- variant (control|shadow:<target_idx>) (small)
- model_id (Redwood catalog id) (bounded)
- quant_profile (enum) (bounded)
- rubric_version (bounded)
- metric_name (bounded set of v1 metrics)
- outcome_class (success|refusal|format_error|timeout|server_error|canceled)

Sean Gallagher: route_id is only safe if we cap. some tenants create routes like candy.

Nadia Rahman: we can enforce: only emit route_id for top N routes by volume per tenant per window, else route_id="__other__". already do similar for some API metrics.

Allison Grant: great, that’s B) with guardrails.

Caleb Johnson: what’s N? 

Sean Gallagher: for beta i’d say N=50 per tenant per day, plus global cap. we can tune later but need a default that won’t torch us.

Logan Wright: agree w/ N=50. also: drop route_id completely if route_count exceeds cap and the system is in “cardinality protection mode”.

Ravi Menon: for routing team, route_id in metrics is helpful. but ok with “other bucket” as long as events still have exact route_id.

Allison Grant: events can keep exact route_id, sampled. metrics gets capped.

Selene Huang: on experiment_id: for shadow experiments we might create a lot of short-lived experiments. metric label might still be ok if we cap active experiments per tenant.

Sean Gallagher: experiment_id is a classic “death by a thousand experiments”. i’d prefer: metrics roll up to rollout_id (canary) OR shadow_policy_id, not per-run experiment UUID.

Nadia Rahman: rollouts have rollout_id already. shadow experiments in console are “experiment” objects though.

Logan Wright: compromise: B) experiment_id allowed only for “active experiments” list with limit (e.g., 10 active per tenant). everything else goes to experiment_id="__inactive__". and we should rate-limit creation.

Allison Grant: ok. write it down as B) with cap.

Marcus Lin: also: do not use customer-provided strings as labels (route_name, prompt_set_name, tags, headers). internal numeric ids only.

Caleb Johnson: re: error_code. if we use raw gRPC / HTTP codes it’s bounded. but upstream model errors can be arbitrary. we should normalize.

Allison Grant: yep. metric label should be error_class not error_message.

Sean Gallagher: C) EVENT-ONLY / NEVER AS METRIC LABEL:
- tenant_id / customer_id
- user_id
- api_key_id
- request_id / trace_id
- request_fingerprint
- prompt_id
- prompt_hash
- any header-derived value
- raw error_message
- tool/function name (could be high-card)
- model_version if it’s a git sha / build id (depends)

Benji Okafor: question on model_version_control/model_version_shadow. our model versions are like "llama3.1-70b-instruct@2026-02-11". bounded-ish but could grow fast.

Logan Wright: right now those are semver-ish + date, still increases over time. ok for events, but for metrics prefer model_id + “pinned_version” boolean? or a small “version_channel” (stable|candidate|custom).

Selene Huang: for quality regressions you often need to know which version.

Allison Grant: we can do: model_release_track (stable|candidate|custom) + model_build (optional) in events. dashboards can filter by model_id + rollout_id to infer version.

Sean Gallagher: and if we *must* have version in metrics, it must be bucketed to the versions currently receiving traffic (top K versions), same as route cap.

Nadia Rahman: we can implement “topK label retention” in metrics emitter. K=20 versions per model_id per region? 

Allison Grant: keep it simple for v1: no model_version as metric label. use model_id only. link from rollout timeline to exact version via rollout config.

Ravi Menon: works for rollouts use-case. for ad-hoc comparisons outside a rollout, events will have version.

Caleb Johnson: token counts + latency are currently proposed as labels in a couple queries (like bucket="0-128" etc). i assume we want explicit buckets rather than raw values.

Allison Grant: yes. B) bucketed labels allowed:
- prompt_tokens_bucket (0-128, 129-512, 513-2k, 2k+)
- completion_tokens_bucket (same)
- e2e_latency_bucket (if needed; but we already do histograms)
But generally prefer histograms/summary metrics not string buckets.

Sean Gallagher: +1. don’t invent new buckets unless we can justify. every bucket is a multiplier.

Logan Wright: action items:
1) i’ll update ADR + ENG-5410 acceptance criteria w/ explicit allowlist + caps.
2) Nadia/Caleb: implement “cardinality protection mode” in emitter (route cap + experiment cap + global cap). if tripped, emit metric shadow_cardinality_dropped_total{reason=...}.
3) Marcus: review event payload fields for quasi-identifiers (fingerprint/prompt_hash) + retention/redaction (separate doc).

Marcus Lin: ack. and we should add a lint/test in CI: metric label set must match allowlist (fail build).

Benji Okafor: +1. we can add validation in policy service too: forbid config that tries to turn on unsafe label emission.

Allison Grant: love it. also: dashboards should *not* encourage filtering by high-card dims. no freeform "route contains" on metric queries.

Selene Huang: for science side: we still need segment filters like “deployment_mode, region, route (top), model_id, quant_profile, rubric_version”. that’s enough.

Sean Gallagher: can we codify final list here so nobody bikesheds later?

Allison Grant: final for v1 (metrics labels):
A) always allowed:
- deployment_mode
- region
- model_id
- quant_profile
- rubric_version
- metric_name (from fixed bundle)
- variant (control|shadow)
- outcome_class
B) allowed w/ guardrails (caps/bucketing):
- route_id (top 50 per tenant/day else __other__)
- rollout_id (bounded by active rollouts)
- experiment_id (max 10 active per tenant else __inactive__)
- prompt_tokens_bucket (coarse)
C) event-only / forbidden in metrics:
- tenant_id/customer_id
- request_fingerprint/request_id/trace_id
- prompt_id/prompt_hash
- headers/custom tags
- raw errors
- model_version/build id

Allison Grant: if anyone objects, speak now :)

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: from SRE

Nadia Rahman: :thumbsup: telemetry. i’ll incorporate into ingestion PR + add dropped_total metrics.

Caleb Johnson: :thumbsup: will adjust dashboard queries accordingly.

Ravi Menon: :thumbsup: routing ok.

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: security ok as long as fingerprint stays out of metrics and events are sampled/TTL.

Selene Huang: :thumbsup: science ok.

Allison Grant: cool. closing loop on ENG-5410 w/ this thread link.

title: #eng-sre: 1768014202
preview:
Allison Grant: pulling this into #eng-sre for eyes. SDK parity is about to change default retry/backoff behavior (429/5xx + network). Need to sanity check load impact + agree on rollout monitoring + rollback triggers.

Ibrahim Khan: thx. current draft defaults: retry on 429, 408, 409, 500, 502, 503, 504 + common network errors. respect `Retry-After` (seconds + http-date). backoff = exp w/ jitter. cap attempts. streaming: no automatic retries after first byte.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on the check. retries are where “helpful” turns into thundering herd. what are we actually shipping as defaults? max attempts and initial delay?

Ibrahim Khan: proposal (aligned across py/ts/go): max_attempts=3 total (1 try + 2 retries). base_delay=250ms, max_delay=8s. full jitter (rand(0, backoff)). for 429, if Retry-After present, use it (bounded by max_delay).

Ethan Park: note platform PR is adding Retry-After on 429 consistently, which should help a lot vs clients hammering.

Sean Gallagher: ok. 3 total is conservative. I’m more worried about *aggregate* retries under sustained 429. do we have an estimate of how much RPS increases during a brownout?

Allison Grant: i did a quick model:
- if 10% of requests see 429 and we do 2 retries, worst-case additional load is ~20% of baseline *if* they all retry quickly.
- with Retry-After + jitter, the peak gets smeared, but we still increase tail load while already hot.
need monitoring/rollback so we don’t repeat the “retry storm” pattern.

Ravi (Incident Bot): (context) There was a suspected client retry amplification during elevated 429s last quarter. Postmortem tag: "client-retry-storm".

Monica Patel: in the SDKs we’re also adding a retry “budget” knob (max_total_retry_time_ms) but not defaulting it on yet. we can keep defaults minimal and document.

Sean Gallagher: i’d prefer we *do* have a default budget to prevent pathological “slow death”. but if that’s too breaking, we need hard rollback.

Ibrahim Khan: budgets are tricky cross-language (different http stacks). defaulting could be surprising. can we instead enforce: overall request timeout already bounds it + max_attempts=3.

Sean Gallagher: overall timeouts help but only if honored everywhere (esp streaming). ok for non-stream.

Allison Grant: let’s define the SRE view: what do we need instrumented so we can quickly say “new SDK defaults are amplifying load”?

Logan Wright: for telemetry/log fields: we can standardize `request_id`, `sdk_language`, `sdk_version`, `retry_count`, `max_attempts`, `retry_reason` (status_code vs network), `retry_after_ms_used`, `backoff_ms`, `timeout_ms`, and for streaming `stream_disconnect_reason`.

Nadia Rahman: dashboard dimensions we can slice by: route (chat/completions/embeddings), model, region, customer_tier, sdk_language+version. we can do log-based metrics in honeycomb + prom counters where available.

Dev Patel: from support side, biggest pain is “it works in TS but not Python” + can’t find request_id. as long as we can correlate retry_count and request_id quickly, support can triage escalations w/out guessing.

Sean Gallagher: metrics i want during rollout:
1) gateway 429 rate + Retry-After distribution
2) request rate (RPS) per route/model/region
3) retries per successful request (avg + p95)
4) attempt-level error rate (5xx, network) and latency (p95/p99)
5) saturation: GPU util, queue depth, admission control events

Allison Grant: agree. also: “time spent sleeping due to Retry-After/backoff” would be helpful to confirm clients are respecting it.

Ibrahim Khan: we can emit `retry_sleep_ms_total` as debug hook / log field (not always metric).

Sean Gallagher: now rollback triggers. let’s be explicit w thresholds.

Allison Grant: draft triggers (during beta / first 72h stable):
- Trigger A: retries_per_request (successful) increases >0.15 absolute vs baseline for >15 min AND 429 rate >2% on same routes.
- Trigger B: total ingress RPS increases >10% while unique client count is flat (proxy for retry amplification).
- Trigger C: p95 latency +20% for >20 min correlated with retry increase.
- Trigger D: any sign of retrying non-retryables (e.g., 400/401) >0.1% of attempts.
If any trigger fires -> pause rollout + cut a patch disabling retries by default (or force max_attempts=1) on affected SDK release channel.

Sean Gallagher: i like A/B/C. D is “correctness” not just load. also want: if we see 429 storms in a single large tenant due to misconfig, we shouldn’t rollback globally—need per-tenant mitigation.

Ethan Park: platform can rate limit per api key + return Retry-After. but SDK behavior still matters. we can also add a gateway-side “do not retry” code flag later, not in this scope.

Monica Patel: re rollout: plan is beta channel first (opt-in flag in docs), then stable defaults. can we do “defaults on” only after 1 week of stable metrics?

Sean Gallagher: yes. and stagger languages. don’t ship py/ts/go defaults flipping in the same 24h.

Connor O'Brien: release coordination suggestion:
- Week 1: Python beta
- Week 2: TS beta
- Week 3: Go beta
- Week 4: Python stable default
…with explicit stop/go gates based on the dashboard.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: +1. also we should pin a runbook in the rollout ticket: what graphs to look at + who’s on point.

Ibrahim Khan: question: should we treat 409 as retryable? current platform uses 409 for idempotency conflict sometimes.

Sean Gallagher: 409 retryable is ok only if we’re confident it’s safe + not a client bug loop. make sure max_attempts=3 applies. log it.

Dev Patel: pls ensure error messages tell customers “retryable=true/false” and include request_id. we’ve had too many back-and-forths.

Logan Wright: for privacy/security: no api keys in logs. redaction enforced. request_id ok.

Nadia Rahman: i’ll add panels:
- retry_count histogram by sdk_language/sdk_version
- rate of `retry_reason=status_429` vs `status_5xx` vs `network_error`
- Retry-After honored ratio (where header present)
- streaming_disconnect_reason counts (even though no retries there)

Allison Grant: on streaming: can we at least make sure we DON’T retry after partial tokens? that’s the scary duplicate side effects.

Ibrahim Khan: correct. standard: no auto-retry after first byte/event. SDK raises typed StreamingDisconnectError w/ request_id + partial_output. docs say: only retry if you can tolerate duplicates + use idempotency keys where applicable.

Sean Gallagher: good. last thing: cap concurrent retries? some clients do fanout.

Ibrahim Khan: per-request only. we’re not implementing global concurrency limiting in SDK right now. but we can recommend app-level queueing. (also why max_attempts is low.)

Sean Gallagher: ok. SRE signoff contingent on:
- dashboards live before stable flip
- rollout gates + staggered releases
- clear rollback lever (feature flag or patch release) documented

Allison Grant: noted. action items:
- Allison: finalize rollback thresholds + publish runbook link in ENG-5131
- Nadia/Logan: dashboard + log fields spec by EOW
- Connor: propose staggered schedule in #eng-releases
- Ibrahim: ensure defaults are max_attempts=3 + honor Retry-After bounded + no streaming retries

Monica Patel: ack. will update the RFC and loop back for final signoff.

Sean Gallagher: :white_check_mark: thanks. ping me when dashboards are up; i’ll do a 10-min review before we flip any stable defaults.

1) gateway 429 rate + Retry-After distribution
2) request rate (RPS) per route/model/region
3) retries per successful request (avg + p95)
4) attempt-level error rate (5xx, network) and latency (p95/p99)
5) saturation: GPU util, queue depth, admission control events

Allison Grant: agree. also: “time spent sleeping due to Retry-After/backoff” would be helpful to confirm clients are respecting it.

Ibrahim Khan: we can emit `retry_sleep_ms_total` as debug hook / log field (not always metric).

Sean Gallagher: now rollback triggers. let’s be explicit w thresholds.

Allison Grant: draft triggers (during beta / first 72h stable):
- Trigger A: retries_per_request (successful) increases >0.15 absolute vs baseline for >15 min AND 429 rate >2% on same routes.
- Trigger B: total ingress RPS increases >10% while unique client count is flat (proxy for retry amplification).
- Trigger C: p95 latency +20% for >20 min correlated with retry increase.
- Trigger D: any sign of retrying non-retryables (e.g., 400/401) >0.1% of attempts.
If any trigger fires -> pause rollout + cut a patch disabling retries by default (or force max_attempts=1) on affected SDK release channel.

Sean Gallagher: i like A/B/C. D is “correctness” not just load. also want: if we see 429 storms in a single large tenant due to misconfig, we shouldn’t rollback globally—need per-tenant mitigation.

Ethan Park: platform can rate limit per api key + return Retry-After. but SDK behavior still matters. we can also add a gateway-side “do not retry” code flag later, not in this scope.

Monica Patel: re rollout: plan is beta channel first (opt-in flag in docs), then stable defaults. can we do “defaults on” only after 1 week of stable metrics?

Sean Gallagher: yes. and stagger languages. don’t ship py/ts/go defaults flipping in the same 24h.

Connor O'Brien: release coordination suggestion:
- Week 1: Python beta
- Week 2: TS beta
- Week 3: Go beta
- Week 4: Python stable default
…with explicit stop/go gates based on the dashboard.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: +1. also we should pin a runbook in the rollout ticket: what graphs to look at + who’s on point.

Ibrahim Khan: question: should we treat 409 as retryable? current platform uses 409 for idempotency conflict sometimes.

Sean Gallagher: 409 retryable is ok only if we’re confident it’s safe + not a client bug loop. make sure max_attempts=3 applies. log it.

Dev Patel: pls ensure error messages tell customers “retryable=true/false” and include request_id. we’ve had too many back-and-forths.

Logan Wright: for privacy/security: no api keys in logs. redaction enforced. request_id ok.

Nadia Rahman: i’ll add panels:
- retry_count histogram by sdk_language/sdk_version
- rate of `retry_reason=status_429` vs `status_5xx` vs `network_error`
- Retry-After honored ratio (where header present)
- streaming_disconnect_reason counts (even though no retries there)

Allison Grant: on streaming: can we at least make sure we DON’T retry after partial tokens? that’s the scary duplicate side effects.

Ibrahim Khan: correct. standard: no auto-retry after first byte/event. SDK raises typed StreamingDisconnectError w/ request_id + partial_output. docs say: only retry if you can tolerate duplicates + use idempotency keys where applicable.

Sean Gallagher: good. last thing: cap concurrent retries? some clients do fanout.

Ibrahim Khan: per-request only. we’re not implementing global concurrency limiting in SDK right now. but we can recommend app-level queueing. (also why max_attempts is low.)

Sean Gallagher: ok. SRE signoff contingent on:
- dashboards live before stable flip
- rollout gates + staggered releases
- clear rollback lever (feature flag or patch release) documented

Allison Grant: noted. action items:
- Allison: finalize rollback thresholds + publish runbook link in ENG-5131
- Nadia/Logan: dashboard + log fields spec by EOW
- Connor: propose staggered schedule in #eng-releases
- Ibrahim: ensure defaults are max_attempts=3 + honor Retry-After bounded + no streaming retries

Monica Patel: ack. will update the RFC and loop back for final signoff.

Sean Gallagher: :white_check_mark: thanks. ping me when dashboards are up; i’ll do a 10-min review before we flip any stable defaults.

title: #eng-sre: 1767982209
preview:
Sean Gallagher: dropping the first cut of SLO/alert proposal for shadow scoring + shadow target health. want eyes esp on paging policy before we lock for beta.

Sean Gallagher: tl;dr: shadowing is *fail-open* so we shouldn’t page on “shadow is broken” unless it’s (a) causing user-path harm (latency/quota), or (b) we’re blind during an active rollout where people expect quality signals.

Allison Grant: +1. we need to separate “customer impact” from “product usefulness”. most of these are usefulness signals.

Sean Gallagher: proposed SLIs (per tenant+route+experiment, but alerts aggregate up to avoid cardinality):
1) scoring lag: time from `shadow_pair_ingested_ts` -> `scores_written_ts`
2) scoring availability: % of shadow pairs that eventually get a score within window
3) shadow target error rate: % mirror attempts that return non-2xx (or transport errors)
4) mirror overhead: added CPU + p99 on router, but that’s on existing routing SLOs

Sean Gallagher: proposal for scoring lag SLO (platform-wide):
- p95 lag <= 5m over 30m window
- p99 lag <= 30m over 30m window
and availability:
- >= 99% of pairs scored within 2h (rolling 24h)

Nadia Rahman: on lag numbers: 5m p95 seems tight if queue backs up during regional spikes. do we have capacity model to support that at 1% sampling on high-qps tenants?

Caleb Johnson: we’ve got the backpressure + circuit breaker now, but if breaker trips, lag will look “good” (because we drop/skip shadow) unless we explicitly track dropped pairs.

Allison Grant: yep. need explicit metric: `shadow_scoring_dropped_total` w/ reason. otherwise we’ll get false green.

Sean Gallagher: agreed. add SLI:
- “shadow scoring completeness” = scored_pairs / ingested_pairs, and separately “mirror completeness” = mirrored_pairs / eligible_pairs.

Cole Summers: also anything that might hit quotas needs clear guardrail. if shadow path is spending customer quota, we should page immediately. but i think we agreed it *must not*.

Ravi Menon: mirroring counts against internal budget, not customer quota. control response is unaffected. we do have a hard cap per route (sampling%) enforced by policy service + router.

Sean Gallagher: cool. then paging is primarily around: (1) user path harm, (2) widespread silent failure during beta.

Selene Huang: from the quality side, if scoring is delayed beyond ~30m, the “canary safety” story degrades. teams will assume it’s live and making decisions. so i’d like a page when lag is bad *while any experiment is running*.

Jonas Weber: +1. especially if we’re advertising “quality deltas alongside latency” in the rollout timeline.

Allison Grant: careful: paging on “some experiment exists” can be super noisy. maybe we gate to “active experiment with traffic above N” and “expected scoring on”.

Sean Gallagher: proposal v2 paging policy:
P0 page: any signal that shadowing impacts user path (router p99 regression > X correlated w/ shadow enabled; or circuit breaker misbehaving causing elevated control errors) -> existing routing oncall.
P1 page (shadow oncall): scoring pipeline lag p95 > 15m OR completeness < 90% for 30m AND there exists at least one “active” shadow experiment exceeding 1000 mirrored requests in last 30m.
Otherwise: ticket.

Nadia Rahman: what about ingestion lag vs scoring lag? if ingestion is behind, scoring can’t catch up and the lag metric might be misleading depending where we measure.

Caleb Johnson: we can compute two lags:
- ingest lag: request_end_ts -> shadow_pair_persisted_ts
- scoring lag: persisted_ts -> scores_written_ts
Then alert on each separately.

Allison Grant: + dashboards should show which stage is the bottleneck. otherwise everyone just blames workers.

Sean Gallagher: yep. alert suggestions:
A) Ingestion lag p95 > 2m for 30m => ticket (page only if >10m)
B) Scoring lag p95 > 15m for 30m with active experiment => page
C) Scoring completeness < 90% for 30m with active experiment => page
D) “No scores arriving” (scores_written_total == 0) for 15m while mirrored_pairs_total > 5000 => page

Ravi Menon: for shadow target error rate: what do we want? targets can be flaky (candidate model) but that’s kinda the point. we shouldn’t treat as infra incident unless it’s systemic.

Sean Gallagher: right. target error is customer-facing *in the console* (quality deltas), but not user-facing. propose:
- warn banner in console if shadow target error rate > 5% (per target)
- ticket if platform-wide shadow target transport errors > 1% (likely routing/network)
- page if transport errors > 5% across >=3 regions for 15m (indicates routing bug/outage)

Allison Grant: also distinguish “model returned 500” vs “router couldn’t connect / timeout”. only the latter is ours.

Nadia Rahman: we already tag error_class = {upstream_http, transport, timeout, circuit_open}. can do alert on transport+timeout.

Selene Huang: should we include “score freshness” in the rollout decision UI? like “data is 45m behind” so PMs don’t panic.

Jonas Weber: +1. even just a badge: “Scoring delayed”.

Sean Gallagher: ok. action items:
- Allison: propose exact alert rules + severities in grafana/alerts repo (include dedupe).
- Nadia/Caleb: add dropped/skipped counters + stage lag metrics so we don’t get false green.
- Selene/Jonas: define what “active experiment” means for gating pages (traffic threshold, feature flag state).

ShadowOps Bot: :memo: Reminder set by Sean Gallagher — “Revisit shadow scoring paging thresholds after dogfood week 1 (look at alert volume + lag distributions)” (in 7 days)

Cole Summers: one more: for Private deployments, paging thresholds might be different / customer-managed. doc should say “SLOs apply to Hosted/Dedicated; Private ships dashboards + recommended alerts”.

Sean Gallagher: good call. will add line in doc + link to Private constraints ticket.

Allison Grant: can we put the runbook link in every alert annotation? esp kill switch + how to verify fail-open.

Sean Gallagher: yes. runbook will include: disable scoring workers, disable mirroring per policy, and confirm control path unaffected. will update before merge.

title: #eng-sre: 1764542210
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to put an SLO around *gate execution latency* for Optimize quality gates. Right now rollout controller is basically “wait for gates”, and when eval-harness queues back up we end up blocking canary pipelines for 30–60+ min. That’s not acceptable as we go wider in Feb.

Sean Gallagher: Proposal: define an SLI “time from gate requested -> terminal state (passed/failed/overridden/timeout)” and set tiered SLOs. If we can’t meet, it should fail with a clear reason (SLO exceeded) and require explicit override (no silent pass).

Allison Grant: +1 on defining the SLI precisely. We should split it into at least two measurements though:
1) queue latency (time waiting for GPU / worker) and
2) execution latency (harness runtime)
Otherwise we won’t know if it’s a capacity issue vs a perf regression.

Sergio Costa: What numbers are we thinking? We’ll need thresholds per tier (smoke vs full) and maybe by region (eu-west has been rough lately).

Sean Gallagher: Initial strawman (happy to adjust):
- Smoke gate: p95 <= 6 min, p99 <= 10 min
- Full gate: p95 <= 35 min, p99 <= 50 min
Hard timeout: 60 min -> terminal “failed_timeout”

Sean Gallagher: And a “queue time” budget inside that (e.g., smoke queue p95 <= 2 min).

Connor O'Brien: From CI/release side: +1 on hard timeout. We’ve already seen deploy pipelines hang because a gate run got stuck in retry loops. If we define a terminal timeout state, rollout can make deterministic decisions.

Connor O'Brien: Question: do we want timeouts to fail-closed (block promotion) or fail-open (allow rollout)?

Paula Mendes: For rollouts: should be fail-closed for promotion. But we could do something like:
- require smoke gate to start canary
- require full gate to promote beyond canary
That reduces “blocked at 0%” incidents while still being safe.

Sean Gallagher: Yep, that matches what I’m thinking. The SLO is mostly to make sure canary isn’t stuck waiting forever. If smoke is fast, we can at least start.

Sahana Iyer: From a reliability perspective: if full gates regularly blow past 35–50 min, we’ll start seeing folks spamming reruns, which makes queue time worse. We should explicitly cap auto-reruns under the SLO budget.

Allison Grant: Agree. Also: if eval-harness does reruns for flake mitigation, the SLI should include that end-to-end time. Otherwise we’re “meeting” SLO while the user waits forever.

Olivia Grant: (eval-harness) For the harness side we can emit two histograms:
- `eval_gate_queue_latency_seconds` (time from job enqueued -> first worker start)
- `eval_gate_runtime_seconds` (worker start -> results posted)
And we can add a derived `eval_gate_total_latency_seconds` too.

Sergio Costa: Cool. For Optimize side we should also emit `optimize_gate_total_latency_seconds` from API request -> state machine terminal state, since some time is outside harness (config validation, baseline lookup, artifact upload).

Sean Gallagher: +1. SLO should be on what the customer experiences (Optimize “gate run latency”), with the harness metrics as drill-down.

Allison Grant: Another nuance: we should track percent of gate runs that hit timeout as an error budget burn. (Timeouts should be rare, not the normal case.)

Connor O'Brien: What alerting do we want? Please not a page every time a single run times out.

Allison Grant: Suggest:
- Page: smoke gate p95 > 6 min for 30m OR smoke timeout rate > 2% over 30m (scoped to prod)
- Ticket: full gate p95 > 35 min for 2h OR full timeout rate > 5% over 2h
- Also: queue p95 > budget for 30m -> capacity signal (could page depending on severity)

Sergio Costa: Works. I’ll wire dashboards with breakdowns by `gate_tier`, `region`, `model_family`, `config_type`, `outcome`. Also a panel for “% time spent queued vs executing”.

Paula Mendes: For rollout controller integration, I’ll make sure we have clear messaging in the UI when a gate fails due to timeout (vs quality regression). Something like: “Gate did not complete within 60m (SLO exceeded). Rollout blocked. Request override or rerun.”

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: Also we should add a runbook entry: “Gate execution latency SLO violation” with steps: check queue, check GPU pool saturation, check retry storms, check region routing.

Sahana Iyer: Re: region routing — can we allow eval jobs to run cross-region for hosted API routes, or does that violate data locality?

Olivia Grant: For now most prompt sets are synthetic / internal, but some customers will use private prompt sets eventually. We should assume locality matters. For SLO we can still improve by scaling workers in-region + prioritizing smoke tier.

Allison Grant: Priority queues are key. Smoke gates should have higher priority than full gates. Otherwise someone kicks off a full suite and starves smoke.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. Also we should reserve a small GPU slice for smoke tier so rollouts can always progress to canary.

Sean Gallagher: Action items:
- Olivia: add queue/runtime/total histograms in eval-harness + tags (tier/region/suite_id). ETA?
- Sergio: draft dashboard + alert rules (page vs ticket), tie to runbook.
- Paula/Sean: align final SLO numbers + semantics (smoke for canary, full for promotion) and post in design doc.
- Sahana/Connor: sanity check retry policy so auto-reruns can’t blow the SLO budget.

Olivia Grant: I can get the harness metrics in by end of week, behind a flag, then enable in staging early next.

Sergio Costa: I’ll start dashboard scaffolding today. Will need the exact metric names by tomorrow morning.

Allison Grant: I’ll write up the SLI/SLO definition in the observability standard doc so we’re consistent about what “gate latency” means.

Talia Benson: I’ll track the action items and schedule a 20-min follow-up next week to lock numbers + paging thresholds before limited prod rollout.

Sean Gallagher: Perfect. Let’s keep SLO tight enough to prevent pipeline pain, but not so tight we page constantly while we’re still scaling capacity. :reaction: :thumbsup:
